title: Emotions and Policy Views
authors: Yann Algan; Eva Davoine; Thomas Renault; Stefanie Stantcheva
definition: Respondents view a wildfire video emphasizing negligence and corporate/government blame, designed to elicit anger about climate change.
mechanism: Anger heightens perceived injustice and responsibility for climate damage, mobilizing support for costly mitigation tools like a carbon tax with rebates.
exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical U.S. federal carbon tax with cash transfers that raises gasoline price by $0.40 per gallon and returns revenues as $600 per adult per year.
outcome_variable: Standardized support for carbon tax with cash transfers (5-point Likert).
evidence_quote: “Anger leads to stronger beliefs in human-caused climate change, increased support for climate policies (e.g., carbon taxes or subsidies for electric vehicles)… Anger treatment has a significant positive effect across all climate indices.”
statistical_or_result_detail: Anger treatment coefficient on carbon-tax support ≈ 0.09 (p<0.10, standardized); ≈ 0.72 (p<0.01) on broader climate policy views index.
notes: Effect estimated in OLS with controls for gender, age, income, education, and 2024 vote.

title: Emotions and Policy Views
authors: Yann Algan; Eva Davoine; Thomas Renault; Stefanie Stantcheva
definition: Respondents view climate-disaster videos highlighting terrifying wildfires without attributing blame, designed to elicit fear.
mechanism: Fear increases perceived danger but, in this design, does not translate into higher willingness to back a carbon tax.
exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical U.S. carbon tax with cash transfers raising gasoline price by $0.40/gal and rebating $600/year per adult.
outcome_variable: Standardized support for carbon tax with cash transfers (5-point Likert).
evidence_quote: “By contrast, the fear treatment has no significant effect on any of these outcomes.”
page_or_section: Section 5.3; Appendix Tables A18 and A20, col.4
statistical_or_result_detail: Fear treatment coefficient on carbon-tax support ≈ 0.07, not statistically significant; no significant effect on climate indices.
notes: Null effect despite strong increase in self-reported fear, suggesting fear is less policy‑mobilizing than anger.

title: Emotions and Policy Views
authors: Yann Algan; Eva Davoine; Thomas Renault; Stefanie Stantcheva
definition: Respondents view serene landscape videos with peaceful music, designed to elicit joy and tranquility.
mechanism: Relaxation and positive affect may reduce perceived urgency or threat, slightly lowering willingness to endorse a costly carbon tax.
exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical U.S. carbon tax with cash transfers raising gasoline price by $0.40/gal and rebating $600/year per adult.
outcome_variable: Standardized support for carbon tax with cash transfers (5-point Likert).
evidence_quote: In Table A20, the positive emotion treatment has a small negative coefficient on support for the carbon tax with cash transfers.
statistical_or_result_detail: Positive-emotion treatment coefficient ≈ −0.07 (p<0.10) on standardized carbon-tax support.
notes: Authors stress that positive emotions do not systematically offset negative ones; this effect is only marginally significant.

title: Emotions and Policy Views
authors: Yann Algan; Eva Davoine; Thomas Renault; Stefanie Stantcheva
definition: Indicator for respondents who voted (or would have voted) for Donald Trump in the 2024 presidential election.
mechanism: Reflects conservative and climate-skeptical ideology, strongly associated with opposition to carbon taxation even after controlling for demographics.
exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical U.S. carbon tax with cash transfers raising gasoline price by $0.40/gal and rebating $600/year per adult.
outcome_variable: Standardized support for carbon tax with cash transfers (5-point Likert).
evidence_quote: In the carbon tax support regression, the Trump coefficient is −0.52*** (Table A20, col.4).
statistical_or_result_detail: Trump voter coefficient ≈ −0.52 (p<0.01), about half a standard deviation lower support than Harris voters, conditional on controls.
notes: Represents partisan divide in carbon-tax acceptability within the U.S. sample.

title: Emotions and Policy Views
authors: Yann Algan; Eva Davoine; Thomas Renault; Stefanie Stantcheva
definition: Indicator equal to one for female respondents; male is the reference category.
mechanism: Women may be more sensitive to household cost increases or skeptical of the specific tax design, resulting in lower stated support.
exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical U.S. carbon tax with cash transfers raising gasoline price by $0.40/gal and rebating $600/year per adult.
outcome_variable: Standardized support for carbon tax with cash transfers (5-point Likert).
evidence_quote: Female coefficient is −0.10*** in the carbon-tax support regression (Table A20, col.4).
statistical_or_result_detail: Female coefficient ≈ −0.10 (p<0.01) on standardized carbon-tax support, conditional on other covariates.
notes: Despite often higher environmental concern, women express lower support for this specific carbon tax design.

title: Emotions and Policy Views
authors: Yann Algan; Eva Davoine; Thomas Renault; Stefanie Stantcheva
definition: Dummy for respondents aged 50–69; 18–29-year-olds are the reference group in regressions.
mechanism: Older adults may be more resistant to new taxes or more focused on short‑term cost burdens, reducing support for a carbon tax with rebates.
exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical U.S. carbon tax with cash transfers raising gasoline price by $0.40/gal and rebating $600/year per adult.
outcome_variable: Standardized support for carbon tax with cash transfers (5-point Likert).
evidence_quote: Age 50–69 coefficient is −0.14*** in the carbon-tax support regression (Table A20, col.4).
statistical_or_result_detail: Age 50–69 coefficient ≈ −0.14 (p<0.01) on standardized carbon-tax support.
notes: Age 30–49 shows near‑zero effect; opposition is concentrated among older adults.

title: Emotions and Policy Views
authors: Yann Algan; Eva Davoine; Thomas Renault; Stefanie Stantcheva
standardized_candidate_label: Anger-inducing climate video (vs no video) – progressive carbon tax
definition: Same anger video as above; here effect measured on support for a progressive carbon tax on high-income people.
mechanism: Anger about culpable actors increases demand for punitive, redistributive climate instruments like a progressive carbon tax on the rich.
exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical progressive carbon tax that would raise gasoline prices by $0.40 per gallon for high-income people only.
outcome_variable: Standardized support for progressive carbon tax on high-income households (5-point Likert).
evidence_quote: Anger treatment has a significant positive effect on support for a progressive carbon tax and on redistributive climate policies (Appendix Table A21).
statistical_or_result_detail: Anger treatment coefficient on progressive carbon-tax support ≈ 0.14 (p<0.01).
notes: Although authors highlight funding for vulnerable groups, support for the progressive carbon tax also rises significantly.

title: Emotions and Policy Views
authors: Yann Algan; Eva Davoine; Thomas Renault; Stefanie Stantcheva
standardized_candidate_label: Fear-inducing climate video (vs no video) – progressive carbon tax
definition: Fear-focused wildfire videos without blame; effect measured on support for progressive carbon tax.
mechanism: Despite increasing fear, the treatment does not significantly alter support for taxing high-income households’ gasoline use.
exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical progressive carbon tax that raises gasoline price by $0.40/gal for high-income only.
outcome_variable: Standardized support for progressive carbon tax on high-income households (5-point Likert).
evidence_quote: “By contrast, the fear treatment has no significant effect on any of these outcomes.”
statistical_or_result_detail: Fear treatment coefficient on progressive carbon-tax support ≈ 0.02, not statistically significant.
notes: Reinforces that not all negative emotions are equally effective at shifting carbon-tax views.

title: Emotions and Policy Views
authors: Yann Algan; Eva Davoine; Thomas Renault; Stefanie Stantcheva
standardized_candidate_label: Trump voter (vs Harris voter) – progressive carbon tax
definition: Indicator for respondents who voted (or would have voted) for Donald Trump in 2024, relative to Harris voters.
mechanism: Reflects ideological opposition to redistributive and climate policies, yielding strong resistance to a progressive carbon tax.
exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical progressive carbon tax raising gasoline prices by $0.40/gal for high-income only.
outcome_variable: Standardized support for progressive carbon tax on high-income households (5-point Likert).
evidence_quote: Trump coefficient is −0.62*** for support of the progressive carbon tax (Table A21, col.1).
statistical_or_result_detail: Trump voter coefficient ≈ −0.62 (p<0.01) on standardized progressive carbon-tax support.
notes: Partisan gap for progressive carbon tax is even larger than for the uniform carbon tax with cash transfers.

title: Emotions and Policy Views
authors: Yann Algan; Eva Davoine; Thomas Renault; Stefanie Stantcheva
definition: Female dummy; male is reference group in regressions for progressive carbon-tax support.
mechanism: Women show slightly lower support for taxing gasoline only for high-income households, possibly due to concerns about policy complexity or skepticism toward targeted taxes.
exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical progressive carbon tax raising gasoline prices by $0.40/gal for high-income only.
outcome_variable: Standardized support for progressive carbon tax on high-income households (5-point Likert).
evidence_quote: Female coefficient is −0.07* for progressive carbon-tax support (Table A21, col.1).
statistical_or_result_detail: Female coefficient ≈ −0.07 (p<0.10) on standardized progressive carbon-tax support.

title: Emotions and Policy Views
authors: Yann Algan; Eva Davoine; Thomas Renault; Stefanie Stantcheva
definition: Dummy for respondents aged 30–49; 18–29-year-olds are the comparison group.
mechanism: Middle‑aged adults may anticipate bearing some incidence of higher gasoline prices despite being above student/low‑income status, reducing support for a rich‑only carbon tax.
exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical progressive carbon tax raising gasoline prices by $0.40/gal for high-income only.
outcome_variable: Standardized support for progressive carbon tax on high-income households (5-point Likert).
evidence_quote: Age 30–49 coefficient is −0.12** in the progressive carbon-tax support regression (Table A21, col.1).
statistical_or_result_detail: Age 30–49 coefficient ≈ −0.12 (p<0.05) on standardized progressive carbon-tax support.

title: Emotions and Policy Views
authors: Yann Algan; Eva Davoine; Thomas Renault; Stefanie Stantcheva
definition: Indicator for respondents aged 50–69 compared to 18–29-year-olds.
mechanism: Older respondents are substantially less supportive of redistributive carbon taxation, likely reflecting tax aversion and lower perceived personal benefits from long-run climate mitigation.
exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical progressive carbon tax raising gasoline prices by $0.40/gal for high-income only.
outcome_variable: Standardized support for progressive carbon tax on high-income households (5-point Likert).
evidence_quote: Age 50–69 coefficient is −0.34*** for progressive carbon-tax support (Table A21, col.1).
statistical_or_result_detail: Age 50–69 coefficient ≈ −0.34 (p<0.01) on standardized progressive carbon-tax support.
notes: Indicates a strong age gradient in favourability toward taxing high‑income emitters.

title: Globally representative evidence on the actual and perceived support for climate action
authors: Peter Andre; Teodora Boneva; Felix Chopra; Armin Falk
geographic_scope: Global (125 countries); carbon-tax validation evidence from United States only
standardized_candidate_label: General demand for more political action on climate
definition: Binary indicator equal to 1 if respondent thinks the national government should do more to fight global warming.
mechanism: Reflects stronger concern about climate change and desire for ambitious policy, which is associated with higher stated support for adopting a carbon tax on fossil fuels.
exact_tax_policy_context: US online validation survey (Andre et al. 2022): support for "a carbon tax on fossil fuels" measured on a four-point Likert scale.
outcome_variable: Stated support for a carbon tax on fossil fuels.
evidence_quote: "Similarly, the general demand for more political action is strongly correlated with demand for specific climate policies, such as a carbon tax on fossil fuels, regulatory limits on the CO2 emissions of coal-fired plants, or funding for research on renewable energy (ρ between 0.49 and 0.59, all P<0.001 for two-sided t-tests, N=1,996; Supplementary Table 3)."
page_or_section: Main text p.254; Methods – Validation; Supplementary Table 3
statistical_or_result_detail: Correlation between general demand for more political action and support for specific policy measures (including a carbon tax on fossil fuels) ranges from ρ=0.49 to 0.59; all P<0.001; N=1,996; US sample.
notes: Carbon-tax item appears only in separate US validation survey; exact correlation for the carbon-tax item is not reported separately but is within the reported ρ=0.49–0.59 range.

title: Carbon pricing in climate policy: seven reasons, complementary instruments, and political economy considerations
authors: Andrea Baranzini; Jeroen C. J. M. van den Bergh; Stefano Carattini; Richard B. Howarth; Emilio Padilla; Jordi Roca
geographic_scope: Global review, with specific examples from Switzerland, British Columbia, and other countries
standardized_candidate_label: Progressive carbon tax design protecting low-income households
definition: Carbon or energy tax designs that shield low-income or energy-poor households from net burdens via rebates, targeted tax reductions, or lower VAT on basic goods.
mechanism: Reducing regressivity and addressing inequality concerns makes the tax appear fairer, increasing stated acceptability and support.
exact_tax_policy_context: General designs of national carbon and energy taxes as discussed in empirical acceptability studies (refs. 70–73).
outcome_variable: stated acceptability and support for alternative carbon/energy tax designs
evidence_quote: A general finding of the literature on acceptability of climate policy, and in particular of carbon taxes, is that people tend to have a strong preference for designs that protect low-income households.
page_or_section: PE Issue 1: Distributional Consequences of Carbon Pricing
statistical_or_result_detail: Summarizes multiple survey and choice-experiment studies on carbon/energy tax design; no single numerical effect size reported.
notes: Underlying studies include Bristow et al. (2010), Brannlund & Persson (2012), Gevrek & Uyduranoglu (2015), Baranzini & Carattini (2017).

title: Carbon pricing in climate policy: seven reasons, complementary instruments, and political economy considerations
authors: Andrea Baranzini; Jeroen C. J. M. van den Bergh; Stefano Carattini; Richard B. Howarth; Emilio Padilla; Jordi Roca
geographic_scope: Global review, with specific examples from Switzerland, British Columbia, and other countries
raw_determinant: Concern about income inequality [in Swiss 2015 energy tax ballot]
standardized_candidate_label: Concern about income inequality (Swiss energy tax referendum)
definition: Voters’ concern that an energy tax replacing VAT would worsen income inequality compared with the status quo.
mechanism: Equity concerns led citizens to oppose an energy tax perceived as regressive relative to the existing VAT, despite its environmental rationale.
exact_tax_policy_context: Proposal in Switzerland (2015) to replace the national VAT with a federal energy tax decided by referendum.
outcome_variable: vote choice in the 2015 Swiss referendum on replacing VAT with an energy tax
evidence_quote: Concern about income inequality is among the main reasons why 92% of voters (more than two million people) in a public ballot held in Switzerland in 2015 rejected a proposal for an energy tax to replace the national VAT.
page_or_section: PE Issue 1: Distributional Consequences of Carbon Pricing
statistical_or_result_detail: Public ballot result: 92% of more than two million voters opposed the energy-tax-for-VAT swap.
notes: Summarizes findings from Carattini et al. (2016) on the same ballot.

title: Carbon pricing in climate policy: seven reasons, complementary instruments, and political economy considerations
authors: Andrea Baranzini; Jeroen C. J. M. van den Bergh; Stefano Carattini; Richard B. Howarth; Emilio Padilla; Jordi Roca
geographic_scope: Global review, with specific examples from Switzerland, British Columbia, and other countries
definition: Energy tax structured so that higher-income households bear a larger net burden than low-income households.
mechanism: Progressive burden-sharing addresses inequality concerns and aligns the tax with prevailing norms of fairness, increasing stated acceptability.
exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical progressive federal energy tax for Switzerland evaluated in a choice experiment alongside the 2015 referendum.
outcome_variable: stated acceptability of the proposed energy tax in the choice experiment
evidence_quote: The authors of this study show, using a choice experiment administered at the same time of the ballot, that acceptability is much higher when a progressive energy tax is proposed, provided that information on its distributional properties are made salient.
page_or_section: PE Issue 1: Distributional Consequences of Carbon Pricing
statistical_or_result_detail: Based on a choice experiment at the time of the 2015 Swiss ballot; no quantitative marginal effects reported in this review.
notes: Underlying experiment reported in Carattini et al. (2016).

title: Carbon pricing in climate policy: seven reasons, complementary instruments, and political economy considerations
authors: Andrea Baranzini; Jeroen C. J. M. van den Bergh; Stefano Carattini; Richard B. Howarth; Emilio Padilla; Jordi Roca
geographic_scope: Global review, with specific examples from Switzerland, British Columbia, and other countries
raw_determinant: information on its distributional properties are made salient
standardized_candidate_label: Salient information on progressive distributional impacts
definition: Explicit communication that the energy tax has progressive effects and protects low-income households.
mechanism: Making distributional impacts transparent corrects misperceptions about regressivity, reducing opposition rooted in inequality concerns and raising acceptability.
exact_tax_policy_context: Information treatments in the Swiss 2015 energy-tax choice experiment that emphasized its progressive incidence.
outcome_variable: stated acceptability of the energy tax under different information treatments
evidence_quote: acceptability is much higher when a progressive energy tax is proposed, provided that information on its distributional properties are made salient.
page_or_section: PE Issue 1: Distributional Consequences of Carbon Pricing
statistical_or_result_detail: Choice-experiment evidence indicates higher support when progressive features are highlighted; exact effect sizes not reported in this review.
notes: Effect pertains to informational framing of the same underlying energy tax.

title: Carbon pricing in climate policy: seven reasons, complementary instruments, and political economy considerations
authors: Andrea Baranzini; Jeroen C. J. M. van den Bergh; Stefano Carattini; Richard B. Howarth; Emilio Padilla; Jordi Roca
geographic_scope: Global review, with specific examples from Switzerland, British Columbia, and other countries
raw_determinant: earmarking carbon tax revenues ... for environmental projects or other issues
standardized_candidate_label: Earmarking carbon tax revenues to environmental or climate projects
definition: Using carbon tax revenues to fund environmental projects, green innovation/diffusion subsidies, or climate-related funds (including adaptation in poorer countries).
mechanism: Aligning revenue use with environmental and climate goals increases perceived legitimacy and fairness of the tax, boosting social and political acceptability.
exact_tax_policy_context: Generic national carbon taxes whose revenues are earmarked for green spending, innovation subsidies, or international climate/adaptation funds.
outcome_variable: public and political acceptability of carbon tax designs with different revenue uses
evidence_quote: Of course, earmarking carbon tax revenues, or revenues of auctioned emission permits, for environmental projects or other issues, can be done to increase the acceptability of carbon pricing.
page_or_section: More than Carbon Pricing; Two Ways of Implementing Carbon Pricing Globally – Carbon Pricing Through a Carbon Tax
statistical_or_result_detail: Authors note that survey-based acceptability studies find higher support when carbon tax revenues are devoted to environmental or climate-related uses.
notes: Includes uses such as innovation subsidies and climate adaptation funds, in addition to domestic environmental projects.

title: Carbon pricing in climate policy: seven reasons, complementary instruments, and political economy considerations
authors: Andrea Baranzini; Jeroen C. J. M. van den Bergh; Stefano Carattini; Richard B. Howarth; Emilio Padilla; Jordi Roca
geographic_scope: Global review, with specific examples from Switzerland, British Columbia, and other countries
raw_determinant: importance of environmental organizations in successfully spreading support for British Columbia’s carbon tax
standardized_candidate_label: Environmental NGOs’ lobbying and communication for carbon tax
definition: Active environmental organizations that advocate for and communicate the benefits of the carbon tax, helping to build and sustain support.
mechanism: Green NGOs’ advocacy and information campaigns counter industry opposition, shape public opinion, and thus increase political and societal support for the tax.
exact_tax_policy_context: British Columbia’s revenue-neutral carbon tax introduced in 2008.
outcome_variable: support and political acceptance of the British Columbia carbon tax
evidence_quote: evidence from British Columbia’s carbon tax stresses the importance of environmental organizations in successfully spreading support for it.
page_or_section: PE Issue 2: Carbon Pricing and Lobbying
statistical_or_result_detail: Descriptive case-study evidence summarized from Murray & Rivers (2015); no quantitative effect size reported.
notes: Highlights interest-group support as a facilitator of carbon tax acceptability and durability.

title: Public acceptability of carbon pricing: Unravelling the impact of revenue recycling
authors: Jeroen Barrez
geographic_scope: Multi-country systematic review (studies mainly from Europe, North America, plus selected countries in Asia, Latin America, Africa and Oceania)
standardized_candidate_label: Environmental earmarking of carbon tax revenues
definition: Using carbon or fuel tax revenues for environmental or climate-related purposes instead of the general budget or non-environmental spending.
mechanism: Creates a visible link between the tax and environmental benefits, increases perceived effectiveness and fairness, and alleviates distrust about misuse of revenues, thereby boosting acceptance.
exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon and fuel tax proposals where revenues are earmarked for environmental or climate expenditures (e.g. green funds) rather than general budget use.
outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support/WTP for carbon or fuel taxes under environmental earmarking versus alternative revenue uses.
evidence_quote: “Substantial and robust evidence in the literature indicates that environmental and climate earmarking can significantly enhance public acceptability across various contexts.”
statistical_or_result_detail: Ranks highest among all revenue options in the consensus ordinal ranking of reviewed studies.
notes: Effect can be sizable but acceptability may still remain limited in absolute terms.

title: Public acceptability of carbon pricing: Unravelling the impact of revenue recycling
authors: Jeroen Barrez
geographic_scope: Multi-country systematic review (studies mainly from Europe, North America, plus selected countries in Asia, Latin America, Africa and Oceania)
raw_determinant: Subsidies to low-carbon technologies and energy efficiency
standardized_candidate_label: Using revenues for subsidies to low-carbon technologies and energy efficiency
definition: Earmarking carbon/fuel tax revenues to subsidize renewable energy, clean technologies, and household or firm energy-efficiency investments.
mechanism: Households see direct, tangible environmental and financial benefits from cleaner technologies, increasing perceived effectiveness and fairness of the tax relative to its costs.
exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon and fuel tax designs where revenues are recycled as subsidies for renewables, clean technology adoption and energy-efficiency measures.
outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support/WTP for carbon or fuel taxes with revenues used for low-carbon and efficiency subsidies.
evidence_quote: “Subsidies to low-carbon technologies (e.g. renewable energy) and energy efficiency have emerged as the most popular in many studies… and among the most popular ones in many others.”
statistical_or_result_detail: Joint top position in consensus ordinal ranking (score 8).

title: Public acceptability of carbon pricing: Unravelling the impact of revenue recycling
authors: Jeroen Barrez
geographic_scope: Multi-country systematic review (studies mainly from Europe, North America, plus selected countries in Asia, Latin America, Africa and Oceania)
standardized_candidate_label: Using revenues for environmental infrastructure investments
definition: Earmarking carbon/fuel tax revenues for infrastructure such as public transport, cycling lanes, district heating, or charging infrastructure.
mechanism: Infrastructure spending provides salient, widely shared benefits and low-carbon alternatives to taxed behaviours, enhancing perceived fairness and practicality of the tax.
exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon and fuel tax proposals where revenues fund public transport, cycling ways, heat grids, charging networks and similar environmental infrastructure.
outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support/WTP for carbon or fuel taxes when revenues fund environmental infrastructure.
evidence_quote: “Investments in environmental infrastructure… [are] recognized in many studies as a popular or the most popular recycling option.”
statistical_or_result_detail: Joint top position in consensus ordinal ranking (score 8).

title: Public acceptability of carbon pricing: Unravelling the impact of revenue recycling
authors: Jeroen Barrez
geographic_scope: Multi-country systematic review (studies mainly from Europe, North America, plus selected countries in Asia, Latin America, Africa and Oceania)
standardized_candidate_label: Using revenues for climate change adaptation spending
definition: Allocating carbon/fuel tax revenues to adaptation measures such as flood protection or other climate-resilience investments.
mechanism: Links tax revenues to visible climate-related benefits, but adaptation may feel less directly connected to emissions reductions than mitigation subsidies, so gains in acceptability are smaller.
exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon tax designs in several countries where revenues are earmarked for climate adaptation programmes.
outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support for carbon taxes with revenues devoted to adaptation versus other green or non-green uses.
evidence_quote: “Earmarking revenue for climate change adaptation… [is] found to be less popular than subsidies to low-carbon technologies and energy efficiency.”
statistical_or_result_detail: Intermediate position in ranking (score 2), above general budget and corporate tax cuts.
notes: Evidence based on relatively few studies.

title: Public acceptability of carbon pricing: Unravelling the impact of revenue recycling
authors: Jeroen Barrez
geographic_scope: Multi-country systematic review (studies mainly from Europe, North America, plus selected countries in Asia, Latin America, Africa and Oceania)
standardized_candidate_label: Using revenues to fund environmental research and innovation
definition: Earmarking carbon/fuel tax revenues for R&D and technological innovation related to climate and energy.
mechanism: Some citizens value long-term innovation benefits, but impacts are less tangible and immediate; hence effects on acceptability vary and can be statistically insignificant.
exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon and fuel tax proposals where revenues finance environmental or energy-related R&D programmes.
outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support for carbon or fuel taxes when revenues fund research and innovation.
evidence_quote: “Funding research and technical innovation is a quite popular revenue recycling option… although one study shows it does not significantly affect public acceptability.”
statistical_or_result_detail: Considered in only three studies; one finds no significant effect on acceptability.
notes: Not included in the paper’s consensus ordinal ranking due to few studies.

title: Public acceptability of carbon pricing: Unravelling the impact of revenue recycling
authors: Jeroen Barrez
geographic_scope: Multi-country systematic review (studies mainly from Europe, North America, plus selected countries in Asia, Latin America, Africa and Oceania)
standardized_candidate_label: Targeted transfers (social cushioning) to low-income and vulnerable households
definition: Using carbon/fuel tax revenues for targeted transfers or benefits to low-income or otherwise vulnerable households to offset higher energy costs.
mechanism: Improves perceived distributional fairness and protects vulnerable groups but can be seen as complex or unfair by others, leading to heterogeneous acceptance across studies.
exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon tax proposals with progressive revenue recycling via targeted transfers or benefits to low-income and vulnerable groups.
outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support/WTP for carbon taxes when revenues are used for targeted social cushioning.
evidence_quote: “This revenue recycling option has generally demonstrated a moderate effect… a closer look into the literature reveals mixed evidence.”
statistical_or_result_detail: Consensus ranking score 2; generally above lump-sum transfers but below top green earmarking options.
notes: Some studies find limited or even negative effects on acceptability.

title: Public acceptability of carbon pricing: Unravelling the impact of revenue recycling
authors: Jeroen Barrez
geographic_scope: Multi-country systematic review (studies mainly from Europe, North America, plus selected countries in Asia, Latin America, Africa and Oceania)
standardized_candidate_label: Uniform lump-sum transfers (carbon dividends) to all households
definition: Recycling carbon/fuel tax revenues as equal per-capita cash transfers or rebates to all households or taxpayers.
mechanism: Can increase salience of compensation and progressivity, but many citizens still dislike the tax or distrust the scheme; support varies strongly across contexts and survey designs.
exact_tax_policy_context: Fee-and-dividend style carbon tax proposals in multiple countries, with equal rebates or cheques to households.
outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support/WTP for carbon taxes with per-capita lump-sum rebates.
evidence_quote: “The impact of lump-sum transfers on public acceptability exhibits high variability… there is substantial evidence that lump-sum transfers are unpopular.”
statistical_or_result_detail: Consensus ranking score −6; among the least preferred options overall despite being most popular in some individual studies.
notes: Real-world evidence from Canada and Switzerland shows only limited impact on acceptance.

title: Public acceptability of carbon pricing: Unravelling the impact of revenue recycling
authors: Jeroen Barrez
geographic_scope: Multi-country systematic review (studies mainly from Europe, North America, plus selected countries in Asia, Latin America, Africa and Oceania)
standardized_candidate_label: Tax rebates or compensation to affected economic sectors
definition: Using carbon/fuel tax revenues to provide tax rebates or compensation to firms or workers in sectors adversely affected by the tax (e.g. coal mining).
mechanism: Compensating firms can be seen as unfair or regressive, while compensating workers facing job losses can attract sympathy; overall acceptability depends on who benefits.
exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon tax designs where revenues are used to refund firms or support employees in carbon-intensive sectors.
outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support for carbon taxes with sectoral rebates versus other revenue uses.
evidence_quote: “Refunding firms does not garner significant support. However, the public acceptability of supporting employees… is relatively high.”
statistical_or_result_detail: Consensus ranking score 2; intermediate preference, above deficit reduction and general budget.
notes: Public tends to distinguish sharply between support for firms and for workers.

title: Public acceptability of carbon pricing: Unravelling the impact of revenue recycling
authors: Jeroen Barrez
geographic_scope: Multi-country systematic review (studies mainly from Europe, North America, plus selected countries in Asia, Latin America, Africa and Oceania)
raw_determinant: Reducing indirect taxes, such as VAT
standardized_candidate_label: Using revenues to reduce indirect taxes (e.g. VAT)
definition: Recycling carbon/fuel tax revenues by cutting broad-based indirect taxes like value-added tax or similar consumption levies.
mechanism: Some view indirect-tax cuts as fair and efficiency-enhancing, while others doubt environmental effectiveness or do not link them to climate goals, leading to heterogeneous acceptance.
exact_tax_policy_context: Environmental tax reforms where a carbon or fuel tax is combined with reductions in VAT or similar indirect taxes.
outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support for carbon or fuel taxes when paired with indirect-tax reductions.
evidence_quote: “Using carbon pricing revenues to reduce indirect taxes… yields mixed effects on acceptability. While some studies identify… unpopular… other studies indicate that reducing VAT rate is the most… preferred revenue recycling options.”
statistical_or_result_detail: Ranks third in consensus ordinal ranking (score 3), but individual studies range from least to most preferred.

title: Public acceptability of carbon pricing: Unravelling the impact of revenue recycling
authors: Jeroen Barrez
geographic_scope: Multi-country systematic review (studies mainly from Europe, North America, plus selected countries in Asia, Latin America, Africa and Oceania)
raw_determinant: Reducing labour taxes (including personal income taxes and social contributions)
standardized_candidate_label: Using revenues to reduce labour taxes
definition: Recycling carbon/fuel tax revenues by lowering personal income taxes and/or payroll/social security contributions.
mechanism: Economists stress efficiency gains, but many citizens distrust promised tax swaps or underestimate behavioural responses, leading to modest or even negative effects on acceptability.
exact_tax_policy_context: Environmental tax reforms combining carbon or energy taxes with reductions in income tax or social contributions in several European countries and survey scenarios elsewhere.
outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support/WTP for carbon or fuel taxes when revenues reduce labour taxes.
evidence_quote: “The evidence regarding the effect of reducing (personal) income taxes on public acceptability is mixed… Concerning reducing social contributions, studies found low… intermediate… and relatively high levels of public acceptability.”
statistical_or_result_detail: Consensus ranking score 1; generally less preferred than green earmarking and indirect-tax cuts, but above deficit reduction and general budget.

title: Public acceptability of carbon pricing: Unravelling the impact of revenue recycling
authors: Jeroen Barrez
geographic_scope: Multi-country systematic review (studies mainly from Europe, North America, plus selected countries in Asia, Latin America, Africa and Oceania)
standardized_candidate_label: Using revenues to reduce corporate taxes
definition: Recycling carbon/fuel tax revenues by cutting business or corporate income taxes.
mechanism: Citizens tend to see corporate tax cuts as unfair, benefiting firms rather than households, and doubt that such use contributes to climate goals, leading to strong opposition.
exact_tax_policy_context: Environmental tax reforms and carbon tax proposals where revenues are earmarked to reduce corporate taxation.
outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support for carbon or fuel taxes when revenues reduce corporate taxes.
evidence_quote: “The literature consistently identifies reducing corporate taxes as a highly unpopular recycling option.”
statistical_or_result_detail: Lowest position in consensus ordinal ranking (score −9).

title: Public acceptability of carbon pricing: Unravelling the impact of revenue recycling
authors: Jeroen Barrez
geographic_scope: Multi-country systematic review (studies mainly from Europe, North America, plus selected countries in Asia, Latin America, Africa and Oceania)
standardized_candidate_label: Using revenues for deficit or public debt reduction
definition: Allocating carbon/fuel tax revenues to reduce public debt or budget deficits rather than recycling to households or firms.
mechanism: Some value fiscal prudence, but many distrust politicians and dislike that revenues are not visibly returned or linked to climate benefits, limiting acceptability.
exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon and fuel tax proposals where revenues are earmarked for deficit or public debt reduction instead of tax cuts or transfers.
outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support for carbon taxes when revenues reduce public debt.
evidence_quote: “Some studies show that reducing public debt garners intermediate levels of acceptability… other studies reveal it is among the least popular options.”
statistical_or_result_detail: Consensus ranking score −4; more acceptable than general budget supplementation and corporate tax cuts.

title: Public acceptability of carbon pricing: Unravelling the impact of revenue recycling
authors: Jeroen Barrez
geographic_scope: Multi-country systematic review (studies mainly from Europe, North America, plus selected countries in Asia, Latin America, Africa and Oceania)
raw_determinant: supplementing the government budget / general budget
standardized_candidate_label: Using revenues to supplement the general government budget
definition: Allowing carbon/fuel tax revenues to flow into the general budget without specific earmarking for debt reduction or particular spending items.
mechanism: Low political trust and absent clear benefits create fears of hidden taxation and misuse, making this the least acceptable revenue option in most studies.
exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon and fuel tax scenarios where revenues are added to the general budget for discretionary spending.
outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support for carbon or fuel taxes when revenues supplement the general budget.
evidence_quote: “Using carbon pricing to supplement the government budget is highly unpopular compared to other recycling options… some studies identifying it as the least preferred option.”
statistical_or_result_detail: Consensus ranking score −7; among the two least preferred options.

title: Public acceptability of carbon pricing: Unravelling the impact of revenue recycling
authors: Jeroen Barrez
geographic_scope: Multi-country systematic review (studies mainly from Europe, North America, plus selected countries in Asia, Latin America, Africa and Oceania)
raw_determinant: perceived fairness… with a specific emphasis on distributional fairness
standardized_candidate_label: Perceived distributional fairness of carbon tax and revenue use
definition: Belief that the carbon or fuel tax and associated revenue recycling distribute burdens and benefits fairly across income groups and social categories.
mechanism: Policies perceived as fair—especially those protecting low-income households and imposing higher burdens on the affluent—are more legitimate, increasing willingness to accept or support the tax.
exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon and fuel tax proposals with varying incidence across income groups and different redistributive designs (e.g. progressive vs non-progressive recycling).
outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support/WTP for carbon or fuel taxes as a function of perceived distributional fairness.
evidence_quote: “The literature demonstrates the significance of perceived fairness on the public acceptability of carbon pricing, with a specific emphasis on distributional fairness, particularly among income groups.”
statistical_or_result_detail: Review concludes that perceived fairness is a key driver; progressive designs are generally preferred.
notes: Includes preferences for higher burdens on affluent households and relief for low-income groups.

title: Public acceptability of carbon pricing: Unravelling the impact of revenue recycling
authors: Jeroen Barrez
geographic_scope: Multi-country systematic review (studies mainly from Europe, North America, plus selected countries in Asia, Latin America, Africa and Oceania)
raw_determinant: Carbon pricing policies can be regressive…
standardized_candidate_label: Perceived regressivity and burden on low-income households
definition: Belief that carbon or fuel taxes impose a disproportionately large financial burden on low-income households relative to their income.
mechanism: Perceived regressivity undermines fairness and legitimacy, increasing opposition unless offset by progressive revenue recycling such as social cushioning or lump-sum transfers.
exact_tax_policy_context: Analyses and survey descriptions of carbon and energy taxes that raise energy prices and may be regressive without compensatory transfers.
outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support for carbon or fuel taxes when they are seen as regressive versus when regressivity is mitigated.
evidence_quote: “Carbon pricing policies can be regressive, especially in the absence of any revenue redistribution… Addressing the potential regressivity… can be achieved through a progressive redistribution of revenues.”
statistical_or_result_detail: Review notes that without redistribution, regressivity can heavily disadvantage low-income households, harming acceptability.

title: Public acceptability of carbon pricing: Unravelling the impact of revenue recycling
authors: Jeroen Barrez
geographic_scope: Multi-country systematic review (studies mainly from Europe, North America, plus selected countries in Asia, Latin America, Africa and Oceania)
standardized_candidate_label: Perceived environmental effectiveness of carbon taxes
definition: Belief that carbon or fuel taxes effectively reduce emissions and contribute meaningfully to environmental goals.
mechanism: When people think taxes substantially cut emissions, they see costs as justified and are more open to flexible revenue use; low perceived effectiveness fuels demands for earmarking and opposition.
exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon tax proposals where respondents receive or lack information about expected emissions reductions and environmental performance.
outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support/WTP for carbon taxes conditional on perceived effectiveness.
evidence_quote: “The preference for environmental earmarking is rooted in the perception that carbon pricing on its own is not effective to reduce carbon emissions… people hardly understand the incentives….”
statistical_or_result_detail: Carattini et al. (2017) show that providing information on environmental effectiveness reduces preference for earmarking without lowering acceptability.

title: Public acceptability of carbon pricing: Unravelling the impact of revenue recycling
authors: Jeroen Barrez
geographic_scope: Multi-country systematic review (studies mainly from Europe, North America, plus selected countries in Asia, Latin America, Africa and Oceania)
raw_determinant: Political trust emerges as an important factor
standardized_candidate_label: Political trust in government handling of carbon tax revenues
definition: Trust that politicians and government will implement carbon or fuel taxes honestly and use revenues as promised.
mechanism: High trust reduces fears that the tax is a revenue grab or that revenues will be misused, making citizens more willing to accept the tax and less insistent on strict earmarking.
exact_tax_policy_context: Surveys and experiments on carbon and fuel taxes in multiple countries, examining how general political trust moderates support for such taxes and revenue uses.
outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support for carbon or fuel taxes across levels of political trust.
evidence_quote: “Political trust emerges as an important factor in explaining attitudes towards carbon pricing… those with high levels of political trust tend to be more positive towards carbon pricing.”
statistical_or_result_detail: Cites cross-national survey evidence (e.g. Fairbrother et al. 2019) showing stronger support among high-trust individuals.
notes: Low trust also strengthens preferences for strict earmarking over general budget use.

title: Public acceptability of carbon pricing: Unravelling the impact of revenue recycling
authors: Jeroen Barrez
geographic_scope: Multi-country systematic review (studies mainly from Europe, North America, plus selected countries in Asia, Latin America, Africa and Oceania)
raw_determinant: salience and communication of revenue recycling
standardized_candidate_label: Salience and information about revenue recycling
definition: Degree to which people are informed about and can clearly see how carbon tax revenues are used and what they receive in return.
mechanism: Clear, credible information about revenue use, transfers and emission reductions corrects misperceptions, raises perceived benefits and fairness, and can shift preferences toward progressive designs, increasing overall acceptability.
exact_tax_policy_context: Survey experiments and real-world cases (Canada, Switzerland) where information about revenue recycling or its magnitude is or is not provided to respondents.
outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support for carbon taxes before and after information on revenue recycling and its effects.
evidence_quote: “Providing information about revenue recycling increases public acceptability of carbon pricing… making the distributional effects salient… enhances the preference for progressive designs….”
statistical_or_result_detail: Cites multiple experiments (e.g. Beiser-McGrath & Bernauer 2019; Jagers et al. 2021) showing positive information effects on support.
notes: Low baseline awareness of existing recycling in Canada and Switzerland highlights importance of salience.

title: Public acceptability of carbon pricing: Unravelling the impact of revenue recycling
authors: Jeroen Barrez
geographic_scope: Multi-country systematic review (studies mainly from Europe, North America, plus selected countries in Asia, Latin America, Africa and Oceania)
raw_determinant: The carbon tax in British Columbia became acceptable over time
standardized_candidate_label: Policy experience and learning from implemented carbon taxes
definition: Exposure to an actually implemented carbon tax over time, as opposed to hypothetical proposals only.
mechanism: As people observe real impacts and revenue recycling in practice, fears may subside and they adapt behaviour, leading to higher acceptance than before implementation.
exact_tax_policy_context: British Columbia’s revenue-neutral carbon tax introduced in 2008, with public opinion tracked over subsequent years.
outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support for an implemented carbon tax over time versus initial opposition pre-implementation.
evidence_quote: “Murray and Rivers (2015) observe that the carbon tax in British Columbia became acceptable over time, despite initial opposition before its implementation.”
statistical_or_result_detail: Cites longitudinal polling evidence from British Columbia indicating rising acceptance post-implementation.

title: Meta-analyses of fifteen determinants of public opinion about climate change taxes and laws
authors: Magnus Bergquist; Andreas Nilsson; Niklas Harring; Sverker C. Jagers
geographic_scope: 33 countries (Africa, Asia, Europe, North America, Oceania)
definition: Perceived fairness of the climate change tax/law (fair treatment and fair cost/benefit distribution, e.g., by need or merit).
mechanism: Fairness perceptions increase perceived legitimacy and equitable burden-sharing, making people more willing to accept climate mitigation taxes/laws.
exact_tax_policy_context: Public opinion toward climate change taxes and laws (including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation, plus related legal/regulatory instruments) aimed at mitigating climate change.
outcome_variable: Public opinion/acceptability toward climate change taxes and laws
evidence_quote: fairness showed the strongest relationship with public opinion (r=0.65, 95% confidence interval (CI) (0.57, 0.71)).
notes: Distributional fairness (r=0.73) >> personal fairness (r=0.17).

title: Meta-analyses of fifteen determinants of public opinion about climate change taxes and laws
authors: Magnus Bergquist; Andreas Nilsson; Niklas Harring; Sverker C. Jagers
geographic_scope: 33 countries (Africa, Asia, Europe, North America, Oceania)
definition: Belief that the climate change tax/law will fulfil its aim (especially mitigating climate change).
mechanism: Perceived effectiveness raises expected benefits and reduces skepticism, increasing acceptability of the policy.
exact_tax_policy_context: Public opinion toward climate change taxes and laws (including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation, plus related legal/regulatory instruments) aimed at mitigating climate change.
outcome_variable: Public opinion/acceptability toward climate change taxes and laws
evidence_quote: effectiveness to be the second strongest determinant (r=0.54, 95% CI (0.45, 0.63)).
notes: Mitigating climate change (r=0.50) > changing behaviour (r=0.30).

title: Meta-analyses of fifteen determinants of public opinion about climate change taxes and laws
authors: Magnus Bergquist; Andreas Nilsson; Niklas Harring; Sverker C. Jagers
geographic_scope: 33 countries (Africa, Asia, Europe, North America, Oceania)
definition: Degree of worry/concern about climate change.
mechanism: Greater concern signals higher perceived stakes, increasing motivation to support mitigation taxes/laws.
exact_tax_policy_context: Public opinion toward climate change taxes and laws (including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation, plus related legal/regulatory instruments) aimed at mitigating climate change.
outcome_variable: Public opinion/acceptability toward climate change taxes and laws
evidence_quote: Climate change concern was strongly related to public opinion (r=0.48, 95% CI (0.37, 0.58)).

title: Meta-analyses of fifteen determinants of public opinion about climate change taxes and laws
authors: Magnus Bergquist; Andreas Nilsson; Niklas Harring; Sverker C. Jagers
geographic_scope: 33 countries (Africa, Asia, Europe, North America, Oceania)
definition: Perceived personal or societal threats to wellbeing associated with climate change.
mechanism: Higher perceived risk increases urgency and perceived need for mitigation, raising support.
exact_tax_policy_context: Public opinion toward climate change taxes and laws (including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation, plus related legal/regulatory instruments) aimed at mitigating climate change.
outcome_variable: Public opinion/acceptability toward climate change taxes and laws
evidence_quote: climate change risk perception (r=0.31, 95% CI (0.19, 0.41)).

title: Meta-analyses of fifteen determinants of public opinion about climate change taxes and laws
authors: Magnus Bergquist; Andreas Nilsson; Niklas Harring; Sverker C. Jagers
geographic_scope: 33 countries (Africa, Asia, Europe, North America, Oceania)
definition: Extent to which climate change is perceived as a serious problem.
mechanism: When climate change is seen as serious, mitigation policies appear more necessary, increasing acceptance.
exact_tax_policy_context: Public opinion toward climate change taxes and laws (including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation, plus related legal/regulatory instruments) aimed at mitigating climate change.
outcome_variable: Public opinion/acceptability toward climate change taxes and laws
evidence_quote: climate change seriousness (r=0.29, 95% CI (0.19, 0.38)).

title: Meta-analyses of fifteen determinants of public opinion about climate change taxes and laws
authors: Magnus Bergquist; Andreas Nilsson; Niklas Harring; Sverker C. Jagers
geographic_scope: 33 countries (Africa, Asia, Europe, North America, Oceania)
definition: Belief that climate change is anthropogenic.
mechanism: Believing climate change is caused by humans strengthens causal understanding and supports mitigation taxes/laws.
exact_tax_policy_context: Public opinion toward climate change taxes and laws (including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation, plus related legal/regulatory instruments) aimed at mitigating climate change.
outcome_variable: Public opinion/acceptability toward climate change taxes and laws
evidence_quote: weak-to-medium-sized relationship between climate change belief and public opinion (r=0.23, 95% CI (0.14, 0.31)).
notes: Stronger correlation in North America (r=0.61) than Europe (r=0.17).

title: Meta-analyses of fifteen determinants of public opinion about climate change taxes and laws
authors: Magnus Bergquist; Andreas Nilsson; Niklas Harring; Sverker C. Jagers
geographic_scope: 33 countries (Africa, Asia, Europe, North America, Oceania)
definition: Subjective or objective knowledge about climate change.
mechanism: More knowledge can improve understanding of risks/causes, modestly increasing policy acceptability.
exact_tax_policy_context: Public opinion toward climate change taxes and laws (including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation, plus related legal/regulatory instruments) aimed at mitigating climate change.
outcome_variable: Public opinion/acceptability toward climate change taxes and laws
evidence_quote: knowledge to be weakly positively related to public opinion (r=0.14, 95% CI (0.07, 0.20)).
notes: No significant difference: objective (r=0.16) vs subjective knowledge (r=0.13).

title: Meta-analyses of fifteen determinants of public opinion about climate change taxes and laws
authors: Magnus Bergquist; Andreas Nilsson; Niklas Harring; Sverker C. Jagers
geographic_scope: 33 countries (Africa, Asia, Europe, North America, Oceania)
definition: Values prioritizing universalism/altruism and biospheric concerns (self-transcendent values).
mechanism: Pro-social/nature-oriented values align with climate mitigation goals, increasing support for taxes/laws.
exact_tax_policy_context: Public opinion toward climate change taxes and laws (including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation, plus related legal/regulatory instruments) aimed at mitigating climate change.
outcome_variable: Public opinion/acceptability toward climate change taxes and laws
evidence_quote: public opinion was positively related to self-transcendent values (r=0.26, 95% CI (0.21, 0.31)).
notes: Weaker in Asia (r=0.07) than Europe (r=0.28) and Oceania (r=0.30).

title: Meta-analyses of fifteen determinants of public opinion about climate change taxes and laws
authors: Magnus Bergquist; Andreas Nilsson; Niklas Harring; Sverker C. Jagers
geographic_scope: 33 countries (Africa, Asia, Europe, North America, Oceania)
definition: Egoistic/self-enhancement values emphasizing authority, wealth, ambition and influence.
mechanism: Egocentric goal priorities can conflict with collective environmental sacrifices, lowering tax acceptance.
exact_tax_policy_context: Public opinion toward climate change taxes and laws (including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation, plus related legal/regulatory instruments) aimed at mitigating climate change.
outcome_variable: Public opinion/acceptability toward climate change taxes and laws
evidence_quote: public opinion was negatively related to self-enhancement values (r=−0.09, 95% CI (−0.12, −0.05)).
notes: Overall relationship is weakly negative.

title: Meta-analyses of fifteen determinants of public opinion about climate change taxes and laws
authors: Magnus Bergquist; Andreas Nilsson; Niklas Harring; Sverker C. Jagers
geographic_scope: 33 countries (Africa, Asia, Europe, North America, Oceania)
definition: Trust in institutions responsible for implementing climate mitigation policies.
mechanism: Trust reduces perceived vulnerability/mistrust, increasing willingness to accept policy burdens imposed by institutions.
exact_tax_policy_context: Public opinion toward climate change taxes and laws (including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation, plus related legal/regulatory instruments) aimed at mitigating climate change.
outcome_variable: Public opinion/acceptability toward climate change taxes and laws
evidence_quote: Results from our meta-analysis found a positive relationship for trust (r=0.23, 95% CI (0.19, 0.27)).
notes: Stronger for trust in implementing institutions (r=0.43) vs political institutions (r=0.21).

title: Meta-analyses of fifteen determinants of public opinion about climate change taxes and laws
authors: Magnus Bergquist; Andreas Nilsson; Niklas Harring; Sverker C. Jagers
geographic_scope: 33 countries (Africa, Asia, Europe, North America, Oceania)
definition: Right/conservative political ideology (vs left/liberal).
mechanism: Right/conservative identification is associated with lower acceptance of climate mitigation taxes, reflecting ideological conflict/polarization.
exact_tax_policy_context: Public opinion toward climate change taxes and laws (including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation, plus related legal/regulatory instruments) aimed at mitigating climate change.
outcome_variable: Public opinion/acceptability toward climate change taxes and laws
evidence_quote: identifying as politically right or conservative (versus left or liberal) was negatively associated with public opinion (r=−0.18, 95% CI (−0.24, −0.13)).
notes: Effect stronger in North America (r=−0.34) than Europe (r=−0.15) or Oceania (r=−0.09).

title: Meta-analyses of fifteen determinants of public opinion about climate change taxes and laws
authors: Magnus Bergquist; Andreas Nilsson; Niklas Harring; Sverker C. Jagers
geographic_scope: 33 countries (Africa, Asia, Europe, North America, Oceania)
definition: Higher educational attainment.
mechanism: Education may increase climate understanding and perceived legitimacy, modestly increasing tax/law support.
exact_tax_policy_context: Public opinion toward climate change taxes and laws (including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation, plus related legal/regulatory instruments) aimed at mitigating climate change.
outcome_variable: Public opinion/acceptability toward climate change taxes and laws
evidence_quote: Education was positively associated with public opinion (r=0.13, 95% CI (0.11, 0.16)).
notes: Not significant in Oceania (r=0.04, P=0.09).

title: Meta-analyses of fifteen determinants of public opinion about climate change taxes and laws
authors: Magnus Bergquist; Andreas Nilsson; Niklas Harring; Sverker C. Jagers
geographic_scope: 33 countries (Africa, Asia, Europe, North America, Oceania)
definition: Older age (versus younger).
mechanism: Older people show slightly lower support/acceptance for climate mitigation taxes and laws.
exact_tax_policy_context: Public opinion toward climate change taxes and laws (including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation, plus related legal/regulatory instruments) aimed at mitigating climate change.
outcome_variable: Public opinion/acceptability toward climate change taxes and laws
evidence_quote: being younger (r=–0.07, 95% CI (–0.09, –0.04)) was related to public opinion.
notes: Age effect significant for economic policies (r=−0.08) but not for regulations.

title: Meta-analyses of fifteen determinants of public opinion about climate change taxes and laws
authors: Magnus Bergquist; Andreas Nilsson; Niklas Harring; Sverker C. Jagers
geographic_scope: 33 countries (Africa, Asia, Europe, North America, Oceania)
definition: Higher income.
mechanism: Higher income is associated with slightly greater acceptability, potentially reflecting greater ability to absorb costs.
exact_tax_policy_context: Public opinion toward climate change taxes and laws (including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation, plus related legal/regulatory instruments) aimed at mitigating climate change.
outcome_variable: Public opinion/acceptability toward climate change taxes and laws
evidence_quote: having higher income (r=0.04, 95% CI (0.01, 0.07)) were related to public opinion.

title: Meta-analyses of fifteen determinants of public opinion about climate change taxes and laws
authors: Magnus Bergquist; Andreas Nilsson; Niklas Harring; Sverker C. Jagers
geographic_scope: 33 countries (Africa, Asia, Europe, North America, Oceania)
definition: Gender (female vs male).
mechanism: Meta-analytic overall effect is close to zero and not statistically significant.
exact_tax_policy_context: Public opinion toward climate change taxes and laws (including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation, plus related legal/regulatory instruments) aimed at mitigating climate change.
outcome_variable: Public opinion/acceptability toward climate change taxes and laws
evidence_quote: Gender showed no significant overall effect (r=0.02, 95% CI (−0.01, 0.05)).
notes: Non-significant overall effect close to zero.

title: Voting on the environment: Price or ideology? Evidence from Swiss referendums
authors: Nicholas Bornstein; Bruno Lanz
definition: Share of votes for left and green parties in the 1999 national election.
mechanism: Proxy for ideological support for state intervention, redistribution, and environmental action.
exact_tax_policy_context: Municipal approval of the 2000 Swiss referendums on taxes on fossil/non-renewable energy.
outcome_variable: Municipal yes-vote share
evidence_quote: the percentage of votes gained by left and green parties is strongly positive and highly statistically significant
statistical_or_result_detail: Positive and highly significant for all three tax votes in the full model.

title: Voting on the environment: Price or ideology? Evidence from Swiss referendums
authors: Nicholas Bornstein; Bruno Lanz
definition: Average communal acceptance rate of the five previous national environmental ballots.
mechanism: Captures habitual pro-environmental voting and local social norms favoring green policies.
exact_tax_policy_context: Municipal approval of the 2000 Swiss referendums on taxes on fossil/non-renewable energy.
outcome_variable: Municipal yes-vote share
evidence_quote: average result of the last five nation-wide environmental ballots ... positive and the marginal effect is relatively strong
statistical_or_result_detail: Positive and highly significant for all three tax votes in the full model.

title: Voting on the environment: Price or ideology? Evidence from Swiss referendums
authors: Nicholas Bornstein; Bruno Lanz
definition: Share of residents aged 18-30.
mechanism: Younger communes were less supportive in these referendum outcomes.
exact_tax_policy_context: Municipal approval of the 2000 Swiss referendums on taxes on fossil/non-renewable energy.
outcome_variable: Municipal yes-vote share
evidence_quote: The coefficient for young voters has a negative impact and is highly significant for all three proposals.
statistical_or_result_detail: Negative and highly significant across all three tax votes.

title: Voting on the environment: Price or ideology? Evidence from Swiss referendums
authors: Nicholas Bornstein; Bruno Lanz
definition: Share of residents aged 60 and over.
mechanism: Older communes appear less willing to bear the taxes' costs.
exact_tax_policy_context: Municipal approval of the 2000 Swiss referendums on taxes on fossil/non-renewable energy.
outcome_variable: Municipal yes-vote share
evidence_quote: For older voters the picture looks similar: ... coefficients are all negative.
statistical_or_result_detail: Negative for all three tax votes; significant for two proposals.

title: Voting on the environment: Price or ideology? Evidence from Swiss referendums
authors: Nicholas Bornstein; Bruno Lanz
definition: Share of the population with higher education.
mechanism: May proxy longer horizons and lower discounting of future environmental benefits.
exact_tax_policy_context: Municipal approval of the 2000 Swiss referendums on taxes on fossil/non-renewable energy.
outcome_variable: Municipal yes-vote share
evidence_quote: the proportion of the population with a higher education is confirmed to have a strong impact on the decision.
statistical_or_result_detail: Positive and highly significant across the three tax votes.

title: Voting on the environment: Price or ideology? Evidence from Swiss referendums
authors: Nicholas Bornstein; Bruno Lanz
definition: Share using a car as main work-related commuting mode.
mechanism: Higher expected fuel-price burden lowers support for the tax proposals.
exact_tax_policy_context: Municipal approval of the 2000 Swiss referendums on taxes on fossil/non-renewable energy.
outcome_variable: Municipal yes-vote share
evidence_quote: use of a personal car as main means to commute has a strong negative impact on approval rates.
statistical_or_result_detail: Negative and highly significant across all three tax votes.

title: Voting on the environment: Price or ideology? Evidence from Swiss referendums
authors: Nicholas Bornstein; Bruno Lanz
definition: Average disposable income per taxpayer, with a quadratic term.
mechanism: Ability to bear tax costs; relationship is nonlinear.
exact_tax_policy_context: Municipal approval of the 2000 Swiss referendums on taxes on fossil/non-renewable energy.
outcome_variable: Municipal yes-vote share
evidence_quote: inverted u-shaped relationship between the average communal income and the acceptance of the projects
statistical_or_result_detail: Positive at low income levels, negative at high income levels; turning point around CHF 18k-20k for two proposals.

title: Voting on the environment: Price or ideology? Evidence from Swiss referendums
authors: Nicholas Bornstein; Bruno Lanz
definition: Share of women in the commune.
mechanism: May proxy gender-linked pro-social attitudes, but effect is not robust after ideology controls.
exact_tax_policy_context: Municipal approval of the 2000 Swiss referendums on taxes on fossil/non-renewable energy.
outcome_variable: Municipal yes-vote share
evidence_quote: the difference between men and women vanishes once ideological variables are introduced
statistical_or_result_detail: Positive in the price-only model; weak/non-robust after ideology controls.
notes: Effect not robust in full model.

title: Voting on the environment: Price or ideology? Evidence from Swiss referendums
authors: Nicholas Bornstein; Bruno Lanz
definition: Share of the commune that is French-speaking.
mechanism: Captures regional cultural and informational context that may shape environmental attitudes.
exact_tax_policy_context: Municipal approval of the 2000 Swiss referendums on taxes on fossil/non-renewable energy.
outcome_variable: Municipal yes-vote share
evidence_quote: The coefficient for the French-speaking proportion variable is negative and highly significant.
statistical_or_result_detail: Negative in the price-only model; signs differ across proposals in the full model.
notes: Specification-dependent across the three tax votes.

title: Voting on the environment: Price or ideology? Evidence from Swiss referendums
authors: Nicholas Bornstein; Bruno Lanz
definition: Dummy for communes with more than 20,000 inhabitants.
mechanism: Urban residents may rely less on private mobility and value environmental quality more.
exact_tax_policy_context: Municipal approval of the 2000 Swiss referendums on taxes on fossil/non-renewable energy.
outcome_variable: Municipal yes-vote share
evidence_quote: the coefficients for urbanity are all positive and statistically significant, although with a small effect
statistical_or_result_detail: Positive in the price-only model; cancelled out after ideology controls.
notes: Effect disappears in the full model.

title: Voting on the environment: Price or ideology? Evidence from Swiss referendums
authors: Nicholas Bornstein; Bruno Lanz
definition: Dummy for communes in the canton of Graubünden.
mechanism: Regional context linked to tourism and hydropower interests.
exact_tax_policy_context: Municipal approval of the 2000 Swiss referendums on taxes on fossil/non-renewable energy.
outcome_variable: Municipal yes-vote share
evidence_quote: communes in this canton were on average between 6-11% more favorable to the proposals
statistical_or_result_detail: Positive and highly significant for all three tax votes.
notes: Regional context effect.

title: Voting on the environment: Price or ideology? Evidence from Swiss referendums
authors: Nicholas Bornstein; Bruno Lanz
definition: Share of workers in fossil-energy industries.
mechanism: Workers in exposed sectors may oppose higher energy prices due to expected output and employment losses.
exact_tax_policy_context: Municipal approval of the 2000 Swiss referendums on taxes on fossil/non-renewable energy.
outcome_variable: Municipal yes-vote share
evidence_quote: regions with a larger proportion of workers in the sectors of fossil energy ... opposed the three proposals more strongly
statistical_or_result_detail: Negative, strongest for the green tax reform.

title: Voting on the environment: Price or ideology? Evidence from Swiss referendums
authors: Nicholas Bornstein; Bruno Lanz
definition: Share of workers in the electricity sector.
mechanism: Employment exposure to higher energy prices reduces support for the tax proposals.
exact_tax_policy_context: Municipal approval of the 2000 Swiss referendums on taxes on fossil/non-renewable energy.
outcome_variable: Municipal yes-vote share
evidence_quote: regions with a larger proportion of workers in the sectors of ... electricity ... opposed the three proposals more strongly
statistical_or_result_detail: Negative and highly significant for the solar and green tax votes.

title: Voting on the environment: Price or ideology? Evidence from Swiss referendums
authors: Nicholas Bornstein; Bruno Lanz
definition: Share of workers in paper production.
mechanism: Energy-intensive industrial exposure increases perceived cost burden.
exact_tax_policy_context: Municipal approval of the 2000 Swiss referendums on taxes on fossil/non-renewable energy.
outcome_variable: Municipal yes-vote share
evidence_quote: regions with a larger proportion of workers in the sectors of ... paper production ... opposed the three proposals more strongly
statistical_or_result_detail: Negative in both specifications; significant for the solar and energy-conservation votes.

title: When worry about climate change leads to climate action: How values, worry and personal responsibility relate to various climate actions
authors: Thijs Bouman; Mark Verschoor; Casper J. Albers; Gisela Böhm; Stephen D. Fisher; Wouter Poortinga; Lorraine Whitmarsh; Linda Steg
definition: Endorsement of biospheric values (strongly believing people should care for nature; caring for the environment).
mechanism: Values increase engagement with climate-relevant concerns and mitigation, but their association with fossil-fuel-tax support is small and varies by country; e.g., negative in Hungary.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for “increasing taxes on fossil fuels, such as oil, gas and coal” (ESS8 item: tax fossil fuels).
outcome_variable: Support for tax on fossil fuels (“increasing taxes on fossil fuels, such as oil, gas and coal”)
evidence_quote: Table 1 (Tax fossil fuels): biospheric values β=0.03 [.02,.04]; authors note biospheric values were strongly negatively related to fossil-fuel-tax support in Hungary.
statistical_or_result_detail: Multivariate: Tax fossil fuels R²=.06; biospheric values β̂=0.03 (95% CI [.02, .04]) controlling for worry and personal responsibility.
notes: Overall effect is positive but the relationship is heterogeneous; one country (Hungary) shows a notably negative association.

title: When worry about climate change leads to climate action: How values, worry and personal responsibility relate to various climate actions
authors: Thijs Bouman; Mark Verschoor; Casper J. Albers; Gisela Böhm; Stephen D. Fisher; Wouter Poortinga; Lorraine Whitmarsh; Linda Steg
definition: Self-reported worry about climate change (5-point scale).
mechanism: Climate worry reflects emotionally engaged threat appraisal; it is associated with stronger policy support for fossil-fuel taxes, both directly and partly via increased personal responsibility.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for “increasing taxes on fossil fuels, such as oil, gas and coal” (ESS8 item: tax fossil fuels).
outcome_variable: Support for tax on fossil fuels (“increasing taxes on fossil fuels, such as oil, gas and coal”)
evidence_quote: Abstract: worry was “both directly and indirectly, via feelings of personal responsibility… associated with climate policy support”; Table 1 shows worry β=0.10 [.09,.11] for Tax fossil fuels.
statistical_or_result_detail: Multivariate: Tax fossil fuels R²=.06; worry about CC β̂=0.10 (95% CI [.09, .11]) controlling for biospheric values and personal responsibility.
notes: Direct positive association with fossil-fuel-tax support is present in the multivariate model; mediation via personal responsibility is discussed.

title: When worry about climate change leads to climate action: How values, worry and personal responsibility relate to various climate actions
authors: Thijs Bouman; Mark Verschoor; Casper J. Albers; Gisela Böhm; Stephen D. Fisher; Wouter Poortinga; Lorraine Whitmarsh; Linda Steg
raw_determinant: Feelings of personal responsibility to reduce climate change
standardized_candidate_label: Personal responsibility to reduce climate change
definition: Self-reported personal responsibility to try to reduce climate change (0–10 scale).
mechanism: Norm activation: perceiving personal responsibility motivates climate action and strengthens support for society-wide measures like fossil-fuel taxation.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for “increasing taxes on fossil fuels, such as oil, gas and coal” (ESS8 item: tax fossil fuels).
outcome_variable: Support for tax on fossil fuels (“increasing taxes on fossil fuels, such as oil, gas and coal”)
evidence_quote: Table 1 (Tax fossil fuels): personal responsibility β=0.17 [.16,.18].
statistical_or_result_detail: Multivariate: Tax fossil fuels R²=.06; personal responsibility β̂=0.17 (95% CI [.16, .18]) controlling for worry and biospheric values.
notes: The paper also argues worry increases policy support partly by increasing feelings of personal responsibility.

title: Public support for climate policies and its ideological predictors across countries of the Global North and Global South
authors: Christian Bretter; Felix Schulz
geographic_scope: Global North: Germany, UK, USA; Global South: Brazil, China, South Africa
definition: Pro-environment biospheric value orientation (e.g., protecting the environment/preserving nature).
mechanism: Stronger environmental values increase perceived moral legitimacy and necessity of carbon-emissions–based taxation.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for the “taxes” climate policy instrument composite (includes a “carbon border tax” on imports based on carbon emissions).
outcome_variable: Policy support for taxes (tax policy instrument composite; 1–6 scale).
evidence_quote: “a positive relationship between biospheric values and policy support (B = 0.27–0.33 … p < .001)”
statistical_or_result_detail: B=0.27–0.33; 95% CI [0.25–0.31, 0.28–0.34], p<.001; association weaker for taxes than for regulatory/info instruments.
notes: Positive predictor for tax support; smaller magnitude for taxes than for other instruments.

title: Public support for climate policies and its ideological predictors across countries of the Global North and Global South
authors: Christian Bretter; Felix Schulz
geographic_scope: Global North: Germany, UK, USA; Global South: Brazil, China, South Africa
definition: Individualistic worldview emphasizing personal autonomy and opposition to government telling people how to live.
mechanism: Individualistic worldviews reduce acceptance of state-led collective interventions such as carbon-emissions taxes.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for the “taxes” composite (including carbon border tax).
outcome_variable: Policy support for taxes (1–6).
evidence_quote: “a negative relationship between individualistic worldviews and policy support (B = − 0.23 to − 0.15 … p < .001)”
statistical_or_result_detail: B=−0.23 to −0.15; p<.001; negative association stronger for regulatory instruments and taxes vs subsidies/info.
notes: Clear negative predictor for tax support across countries.

title: Public support for climate policies and its ideological predictors across countries of the Global North and Global South
authors: Christian Bretter; Felix Schulz
geographic_scope: Global North: Germany, UK, USA; Global South: Brazil, China, South Africa
definition: Trust in government/public authorities (composite across four public institutions).
mechanism: Higher trust increases confidence that carbon tax policy will be implemented in the public interest, raising support.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for the “taxes” composite (includes carbon border tax).
outcome_variable: Policy support for taxes (1–6).
evidence_quote: “a positive relationship between trust in public institutions and policy support (B = 0.11–0.20 … p < .001)”
statistical_or_result_detail: B=0.11–0.20; 95% CI [0.09–0.18, 0.12–0.22], p<.001; stronger association for regulatory instruments and taxes vs subsidies/info.
notes: Positive predictor for tax support; stronger for taxes than for subsidies/info.

title: Public support for climate policies and its ideological predictors across countries of the Global North and Global South
authors: Christian Bretter; Felix Schulz
geographic_scope: Global North: Germany, UK, USA; Global South: Brazil, China, South Africa
definition: Self-assessed understanding of aspects/causes of climate change.
mechanism: More perceived knowledge increases perceived relevance and informed support for carbon-emissions–based taxes.
