exact_tax_policy_context: Support for the “taxes” composite (including carbon border tax).
outcome_variable: Policy support for taxes (1–6).
evidence_quote: “positive association between policy support and self-reported climate change knowledge (B = 0.08–0.12 … p < .001)”
notes: Positive association for tax support in reported models.

title: Public support for climate policies and its ideological predictors across countries of the Global North and Global South
authors: Christian Bretter; Felix Schulz
geographic_scope: Global North: Germany, UK, USA; Global South: Brazil, China, South Africa
definition: Belief that preserving the free-market system is more important than localized environmental concerns.
mechanism: Free-market ideology conflicts with price-based/state-imposed carbon taxation, lowering acceptance.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for the “taxes” composite (including carbon border tax).
outcome_variable: Policy support for taxes (1–6).
evidence_quote: “negative relationship between policy support and free-market beliefs (B = − 0.46 to − 0.38 … p < .001)”
statistical_or_result_detail: B=−0.46 to −0.38; 95% CI [−0.49 to −0.41, −0.44 to −0.36], p<.001; negative association reported across instruments with strongest effects for regulation.
notes: Entered as an additional covariate in the hierarchical regression, so extracted here as a political control/predictor.

title: Public support for climate policies and its ideological predictors across countries of the Global North and Global South
authors: Christian Bretter; Felix Schulz
geographic_scope: Global North: Germany, UK, USA; Global South: Brazil, China, South Africa
raw_determinant: aggregated region (Global North versus Global South)
standardized_candidate_label: Global South vs Global North (tax support)
definition: Whether respondents are in Global South or Global North countries.
mechanism: North–South context shifts baseline acceptability of carbon-emissions–based tax proposals (e.g., carbon border tax).
exact_tax_policy_context: Aggregate mean support for the “taxes” policy instrument composite (includes carbon border tax).
outcome_variable: Policy support for taxes (1–6).
evidence_quote: “Global Southern countries showed stronger support … for taxes (F(1, 11,962) = 1234.93, p < .001)”
statistical_or_result_detail: Region main effect on taxes support: F(1,11,962)=1234.93, p<.001, ηp²=0.09.
notes: Global South support for taxes is consistently higher.

title: Public support for climate policies and its ideological predictors across countries of the Global North and Global South
authors: Christian Bretter; Felix Schulz
geographic_scope: Global North: Germany, UK, USA; Global South: Brazil, China, South Africa
definition: Interaction showing how the individualism–tax-support relationship differs between Global North and Global South.
mechanism: In Global North contexts, individualistic worldviews correspond more strongly to lower acceptance of carbon taxes.
exact_tax_policy_context: Interaction effects on policy support across policy types including the taxes composite.
outcome_variable: Policy support for taxes (within the modeled interaction).
evidence_quote: “individualistic worldviews were more strongly (and negatively) associated … in our Global Northern countries … (p < .001)”
statistical_or_result_detail: Stronger negative individualism association with policy support in Global North vs Global South across policy types incl. taxes (p<.001).
notes: Effect modification; direction remains negative.

title: Public support for climate policies and its ideological predictors across countries of the Global North and Global South
authors: Christian Bretter; Felix Schulz
geographic_scope: Global North: Germany, UK, USA; Global South: Brazil, China, South Africa
raw_determinant: trust in public institutions × aggregated region
definition: Interaction indicating how the trust–tax-support relationship differs between Global North and Global South.
mechanism: Trust is a more important driver of acceptance of carbon tax proposals in Global North contexts.
exact_tax_policy_context: Interaction effects on policy support across policy types including the taxes composite.
outcome_variable: Policy support for taxes (within the modeled interaction).
evidence_quote: “trust in public institutions was more strongly (and positively) associated … in our Global Northern countries … (p < .001)”
statistical_or_result_detail: Stronger positive trust association with policy support in Global North vs Global South across policy types incl. taxes (p<.001).
notes: Effect modification; direction remains positive.

title: Public support for climate policies and its ideological predictors across countries of the Global North and Global South
authors: Christian Bretter; Felix Schulz
geographic_scope: Global North: Germany, UK, USA; Global South: Brazil, China, South Africa
standardized_candidate_label: Biospheric values × Global region (tax support moderation)
definition: Interaction indicating differential strength of the biospheric-values association with support for the taxes composite between Global North and Global South (exception for tax support).
mechanism: Regional/contextual factors condition how strongly pro-environment values translate into support for carbon-emissions–based taxation.
exact_tax_policy_context: Interaction in models of support for the “taxes” composite (includes carbon border tax).
outcome_variable: Policy support for taxes (1–6).
evidence_quote: “…no interaction with biospheric values (with exception of tax support; see Tables 10–12…)”
notes: Main text does not specify which region has the stronger biospheric-values–tax-support slope; direction left as n.a.

title: Denial and distrust: explaining the partisan climate gap
authors: Dylan Bugden
definition: Trust in the scientific community on issues related to climate change (definitely/probably/not sure/probably not/definitely not).
mechanism: Higher trust in scientific expertise increases perceived credibility/legitimacy of climate claims, raising willingness to support policies like a carbon tax.
exact_tax_policy_context: A carbon tax on energy companies and utilities that will raise the cost of using fossil fuels and incentivize use of renewable energy sources.
outcome_variable: Support for the carbon tax policy (support vs oppose/not sure).
evidence_quote: Table 3 (Carbon tax; endowments): Trust (science) = −0.13***.
statistical_or_result_detail: Endowment coefficient (Republicans − Democrats gap): trust(science) = −0.13*** (SE ≈ 0.02). Total explained by observed covariates for carbon tax = 54.57%.
notes: Coefficient is negative because the decomposed gap is defined as (Republicans − Democrats). Substantively, higher trust (more common among Democrats) aligns with higher carbon-tax support.

title: Denial and distrust: explaining the partisan climate gap
authors: Dylan Bugden
standardized_candidate_label: Climate-science denial / low acceptance of climate evidence
definition: Beliefs about whether climate change is occurring, combining yes/no and certainty; operationalized as climate-science denial in the decomposition.
mechanism: More denial/less acceptance reduces perceived need for mitigation and lowers support for a carbon tax designed to reduce fossil-fuel use.
exact_tax_policy_context: A carbon tax on energy companies and utilities that will raise the cost of using fossil fuels and incentivize use of renewable energy sources.
outcome_variable: Support for the carbon tax policy (support vs oppose/not sure).
evidence_quote: Table 3 (Carbon tax; endowments): Climate denial = −0.12***.
statistical_or_result_detail: Endowment coefficient (Republicans − Democrats gap): climate denial = −0.12*** (SE ≈ 0.02). Total explained by observed covariates for carbon tax = 54.57%.
notes: Sign reflects the decomposed gap orientation (Republicans minus Democrats). Substantively, more denial (more common among Republicans) corresponds to lower carbon-tax support.

title: Denial and distrust: explaining the partisan climate gap
authors: Dylan Bugden
definition: Trust in government on issues related to climate change (definitely/probably/not sure/probably not/definitely not).
mechanism: Higher trust in government increases confidence that climate policy would be implemented effectively and with legitimacy, supporting carbon-tax approval.
exact_tax_policy_context: A carbon tax on energy companies and utilities that will raise the cost of using fossil fuels and incentivize use of renewable energy sources.
outcome_variable: Support for the carbon tax policy (support vs oppose/not sure).
evidence_quote: Table 3 (Carbon tax; endowments): Trust (gov’t) = −0.01**.
statistical_or_result_detail: Endowment coefficient (Republicans − Democrats gap): trust(gov’t) = −0.01**. Total explained by observed covariates for carbon tax = 54.57%.
notes: Negative sign reflects (Republicans − Democrats) gap definition; substantively, greater government trust aligns with higher carbon-tax support, though the effect is small.

title: Green Taxes in a Post-Paris World: Are Millions of Nays Inevitable?
authors: Stefano Carattini; Andrea Baranzini; Philippe Thalmann; Frédéric Varone; Frank Vöhringer
definition: Agreement that implementing the energy tax would harm firms’ competitiveness (e.g., higher costs and industry losses).
mechanism: Perceived competitiveness losses reduce support for the energy/carbon tax reform.
exact_tax_policy_context: Swiss Green Liberal popular initiative replacing VAT with an energy tax on non-renewable energy (vote March 8, 2015).
outcome_variable: Yes-vote on the initiative (probit among ballot participants).
evidence_quote: Competitiveness effects −0.394** (0.094)
statistical_or_result_detail: Probit incl. pros/cons: coefficient −0.394**; marginal effect −0.014**.
notes: Core opposition argument in the real ballot.

title: Green Taxes in a Post-Paris World: Are Millions of Nays Inevitable?
authors: Stefano Carattini; Andrea Baranzini; Philippe Thalmann; Frédéric Varone; Frank Vöhringer
definition: Agreement that the energy-tax proposal would have regressive distributional impacts, burdening lower-income households more.
mechanism: Perceived unfair distribution lowers support for the tax reform.
exact_tax_policy_context: Swiss Green Liberal popular initiative replacing VAT with an energy tax on non-renewable energy (vote March 8, 2015).
outcome_variable: Yes-vote on the initiative (probit among ballot participants).
evidence_quote: Regressive effects −0.165* (0.089)
statistical_or_result_detail: Probit incl. pros/cons: coefficient −0.165*; marginal effect −0.006*.
notes: Distributional concerns reduce the probability of voting yes.

title: Green Taxes in a Post-Paris World: Are Millions of Nays Inevitable?
authors: Stefano Carattini; Andrea Baranzini; Philippe Thalmann; Frédéric Varone; Frank Vöhringer
definition: Agreement that the energy tax would create incentives to save energy and switch to cleaner/renewable sources.
mechanism: Belief in environmental effectiveness increases the probability of voting yes.
exact_tax_policy_context: Swiss Green Liberal popular initiative replacing VAT with an energy tax on non-renewable energy (vote March 8, 2015).
outcome_variable: Yes-vote on the initiative (probit among ballot participants).
evidence_quote: Environmental effectiveness 0.378*** (0.110)
statistical_or_result_detail: Probit incl. pros/cons: coefficient 0.378***; marginal effect 0.013**.
notes: Perceived environmental effectiveness is a strong positive predictor of support.

title: Green Taxes in a Post-Paris World: Are Millions of Nays Inevitable?
authors: Stefano Carattini; Andrea Baranzini; Philippe Thalmann; Frédéric Varone; Frank Vöhringer
definition: Agreement that the initiative is revenue neutral (overall tax burden unchanged).
mechanism: Perceived fiscal neutrality reduces resistance by alleviating fears of net tax hikes.
exact_tax_policy_context: Swiss Green Liberal popular initiative replacing VAT with an energy tax on non-renewable energy (vote March 8, 2015).
outcome_variable: Yes-vote on the initiative (probit among ballot participants).
evidence_quote: Revenue neutrality 0.617*** (0.103)
statistical_or_result_detail: Probit incl. pros/cons: coefficient 0.617***; marginal effect 0.022**.
notes: Higher agreement with revenue neutrality increases likelihood of a yes vote.

title: Green Taxes in a Post-Paris World: Are Millions of Nays Inevitable?
authors: Stefano Carattini; Andrea Baranzini; Philippe Thalmann; Frédéric Varone; Frank Vöhringer
definition: Agreement that the energy tax would increase energy security by reducing dependence on oil/gas exporters.
mechanism: Perceived co-benefits raise support for the proposed energy tax.
exact_tax_policy_context: Swiss Green Liberal popular initiative replacing VAT with an energy tax on non-renewable energy (vote March 8, 2015).
outcome_variable: Yes-vote on the initiative (probit among ballot participants).
evidence_quote: Energy security 0.181* (0.108)
statistical_or_result_detail: Probit incl. pros/cons: coefficient 0.181*; marginal effect 0.007*.
notes: Energy-security expectations modestly increase support.

title: Green Taxes in a Post-Paris World: Are Millions of Nays Inevitable?
authors: Stefano Carattini; Andrea Baranzini; Philippe Thalmann; Frédéric Varone; Frank Vöhringer
definition: Household car ownership, proxy for sensitivity to higher fuel/energy prices from the proposed energy tax.
mechanism: More cars imply greater expected fuel-cost burden, reducing support.
exact_tax_policy_context: Swiss Green Liberal popular initiative replacing VAT with an energy tax on non-renewable energy (vote March 8, 2015).
outcome_variable: Yes-vote on the initiative (probit among ballot participants).
evidence_quote: Number of cars in household −0.457** (0.132)
statistical_or_result_detail: Probit incl. pros/cons: coefficient −0.457**; marginal effect −0.016**.
notes: Socioeconomic covariate proxying cost exposure; relevant but not a policy mechanism/attribute.

title: Green Taxes in a Post-Paris World: Are Millions of Nays Inevitable?
authors: Stefano Carattini; Andrea Baranzini; Philippe Thalmann; Frédéric Varone; Frank Vöhringer
definition: Concern about public intervention in the economy (anti-government-intervention attitude).
mechanism: Greater skepticism toward state intervention reduces support for tax-based reform.
exact_tax_policy_context: Swiss Green Liberal popular initiative replacing VAT with an energy tax on non-renewable energy (vote March 8, 2015).
outcome_variable: Yes-vote on the initiative (probit among ballot participants).
evidence_quote: Concern with public intervention −0.085* (0.059)
statistical_or_result_detail: Probit incl. pros/cons: coefficient −0.085*; marginal effect −0.003*.
notes: Attitude control rather than a tax-effect belief.

title: Green Taxes in a Post-Paris World: Are Millions of Nays Inevitable?
authors: Stefano Carattini; Andrea Baranzini; Philippe Thalmann; Frédéric Varone; Frank Vöhringer
definition: Declared affinity/support for green parties (Green Liberals/Green Party).
mechanism: Ideological alignment increases the likelihood of voting yes.
exact_tax_policy_context: Swiss Green Liberal popular initiative replacing VAT with an energy tax on non-renewable energy (vote March 8, 2015).
outcome_variable: Yes-vote on the initiative (probit among ballot participants).
evidence_quote: Green affinity 0.628** (0.260)
statistical_or_result_detail: Probit incl. pros/cons: coefficient 0.628**; marginal effect 0.022**.
notes: Partisan/ideological covariate; relevant but not a tax-effect belief.

title: Green Taxes in a Post-Paris World: Are Millions of Nays Inevitable?
authors: Stefano Carattini; Andrea Baranzini; Philippe Thalmann; Frédéric Varone; Frank Vöhringer
definition: Carbon tax level (CHF per ton CO2) in the choice experiment (0 vs 60/90/120/150).
mechanism: Higher rates raise expected energy prices and costs, lowering acceptability.
exact_tax_policy_context: Choice experiment: carbon tax at 60–150 CHF/tCO2 with specified revenue recycling options vs 0 CHF status quo.
outcome_variable: Acceptability of proposed carbon-tax designs in the choice experiment.
statistical_or_result_detail: Conditional logit (ref 0 CHF): 60CHF −0.018 (ns); 90CHF −0.052***; 120CHF −0.107***; 150CHF −0.177***.
notes: Strongly negative for rates ≥90 CHF/tCO2; effect is small at 60 CHF.

title: Green Taxes in a Post-Paris World: Are Millions of Nays Inevitable?
authors: Stefano Carattini; Andrea Baranzini; Philippe Thalmann; Frédéric Varone; Frank Vöhringer
definition: Use of carbon tax revenues for lump-sum transfers to households.
mechanism: Progressive transfers offset distributional burdens, increasing carbon-tax acceptability.
exact_tax_policy_context: Choice experiment: carbon tax with revenue recycling options including lump-sum transfers.
outcome_variable: Acceptability of proposed carbon-tax designs in the choice experiment.
evidence_quote: Lump-sum redistribution 0.137*** (0.012)
statistical_or_result_detail: Conditional logit (ref income-tax rebate): +0.137*** (SE 0.012).
notes: Strongly preferred when distributional impacts are salient.

title: Green Taxes in a Post-Paris World: Are Millions of Nays Inevitable?
authors: Stefano Carattini; Andrea Baranzini; Philippe Thalmann; Frédéric Varone; Frank Vöhringer
definition: Use of carbon tax revenues for social cushioning/social benefits to protect households from burdens.
mechanism: Mitigates regressive energy-price impacts, increasing acceptability.
exact_tax_policy_context: Choice experiment: carbon tax with revenue recycling options including social cushioning.
outcome_variable: Acceptability of proposed carbon-tax designs in the choice experiment.
evidence_quote: Social cushioning 0.144*** (0.011)
statistical_or_result_detail: Conditional logit (ref income-tax rebate): +0.144*** (SE 0.011).
notes: Distributional protection via social benefits raises support.

title: Green Taxes in a Post-Paris World: Are Millions of Nays Inevitable?
authors: Stefano Carattini; Andrea Baranzini; Philippe Thalmann; Frédéric Varone; Frank Vöhringer
definition: Use of carbon tax revenues to purchase international carbon offsets/additional emission abatements (environmental earmarking).
mechanism: Given effectiveness is communicated, offset-based earmarking loses appeal; voters may also dislike foreign credits.
exact_tax_policy_context: Choice experiment: carbon tax with environmental earmarking via purchase of international offsets.
outcome_variable: Acceptability of proposed carbon-tax designs in the choice experiment.
evidence_quote: Environmental recycling −0.021 (0.013)
statistical_or_result_detail: Conditional logit: full sample −0.021 (ns); nationals −0.029*; without no-voters −0.035**; always voters −0.056**.
notes: Environmental earmarking through offsets is not popular, especially when voters are informed about effectiveness.

title: Green Taxes in a Post-Paris World: Are Millions of Nays Inevitable?
authors: Stefano Carattini; Andrea Baranzini; Philippe Thalmann; Frédéric Varone; Frank Vöhringer
definition: Belief that declining energy tax base would imply higher future tax rates and larger burdens on prices/competitiveness.
mechanism: Anticipated future increases in effective energy prices reduce support for the reform.
exact_tax_policy_context: Swiss Green Liberal popular initiative replacing VAT with an energy tax on non-renewable energy (vote March 8, 2015).
outcome_variable: Yes-vote on the initiative (probit among ballot participants).
evidence_quote: Decreasing tax base −0.057 (0.099) (marginal effect −0.002)
statistical_or_result_detail: Probit incl. pros/cons: coefficient −0.057 (ns); marginal effect −0.002 (ns).
exclusion_reason_if_not_main: Not statistically significant in Table 4 (as noted by the paper).
notes: A theoretically relevant opposition argument but weak empirical signal in the reported model.

title: Green Taxes in a Post-Paris World: Are Millions of Nays Inevitable?
authors: Stefano Carattini; Andrea Baranzini; Philippe Thalmann; Frédéric Varone; Frank Vöhringer
definition: Use of carbon tax revenues to reduce the existing value-added tax (VAT).
mechanism: Perceived as an unrequested/poorly understood tax-substitution design and fails to improve acceptability relative to income-tax rebate.
exact_tax_policy_context: Choice experiment: carbon tax with revenue recycling options including VAT reduction (vs income-tax rebate reference).
outcome_variable: Acceptability of proposed carbon-tax designs in the choice experiment.
evidence_quote: VAT reduction −0.013 (0.011)
statistical_or_result_detail: Conditional logit (ref income-tax rebate): −0.013 (ns).
exclusion_reason_if_not_main: Effect is not statistically significant in the full-sample Table 6 estimate.
notes: The paper interprets VAT reduction as unpopular, but the point estimate is small and not significant in Table 6.

title: How to win public support for a global carbon tax
authors: Stefano Carattini; Steffen Kallbekken; Anton Orlov
geographic_scope: Australia, India, South Africa, United Kingdom, United States (survey); global revenue-use designs
raw_determinant: Funding mitigation projects worldwide received the highest support (65%), on average.
standardized_candidate_label: Earmark revenues for mitigation projects in all countries/worldwide
definition: Using carbon-tax revenues to finance climate mitigation projects in all countries/worldwide.
mechanism: Global spending makes the tax feel effective and mutually beneficial; respondents expect emissions cuts beyond their own country.
exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical global carbon tax (after 2020); respondents asked about support for their own country if all other countries implement; revenue earmarked for worldwide mitigation projects.
outcome_variable: Support for a global carbon tax under the specified revenue-use design.
evidence_quote: Funding mitigation projects worldwide received the highest support (65%), on average.
statistical_or_result_detail: 65% average support for worldwide mitigation (highest).
notes: Revenue-use treatment; highest of tested options.

title: How to win public support for a global carbon tax
authors: Stefano Carattini; Steffen Kallbekken; Anton Orlov
geographic_scope: Australia, India, South Africa, United Kingdom, United States (survey); global revenue-use designs
standardized_candidate_label: Use revenues to lower domestic income taxes
definition: Recycling carbon-tax revenues into lower domestic income taxes.
mechanism: Provides a fiscal offset that reduces perceived net tax burden and household costs, increasing willingness to support.
exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical global carbon tax; survey compares support across revenue uses including lowering domestic income taxes.
outcome_variable: Support for a global carbon tax under the specified revenue-use design.
evidence_quote: These were: lowering income taxes, redistributing revenues domestically to each citizen, and earmarking funds for mitigation projects in all countries.
statistical_or_result_detail: Majority (>50%) support in all five countries (averaged across $40/$60/$80).
notes: One of three designs with majority support in all countries (averaged across tax rates).

title: How to win public support for a global carbon tax
authors: Stefano Carattini; Steffen Kallbekken; Anton Orlov
geographic_scope: Australia, India, South Africa, United Kingdom, United States (survey); global revenue-use designs
raw_determinant: redistributing revenues domestically to each citizen
standardized_candidate_label: Redistribute revenues domestically as per-citizen dividends
definition: Redistributing carbon-tax revenues domestically to each citizen (national per-capita dividend).
mechanism: Direct compensation aligns personal costs with personal benefits, improving fairness perceptions and raising support.
exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical global carbon tax; survey compares support when revenues are returned to residents via domestic per-citizen redistribution.
outcome_variable: Support for a global carbon tax under the specified revenue-use design.
evidence_quote: These were: lowering income taxes, redistributing revenues domestically to each citizen, and earmarking funds for mitigation projects in all countries.
statistical_or_result_detail: Majority (>50%) support in all five countries (averaged across $40/$60/$80).
notes: Domestic redistribution option was one of the three majority-supported designs.

title: How to win public support for a global carbon tax
authors: Stefano Carattini; Steffen Kallbekken; Anton Orlov
geographic_scope: Australia, India, South Africa, United Kingdom, United States (survey); global revenue-use designs
raw_determinant: Sharing out funds per capita globally also appealed ... but not in the United States (44%).
definition: Pooling carbon-tax revenues globally and paying per-capita dividends internationally.
mechanism: Creates international distributional winners and losers; support is higher where dividends are attractive and lower where net contributions dominate.
exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical global carbon tax; respondents evaluated support when carbon-tax revenues are paid out per capita globally.
outcome_variable: Support for a global carbon tax under the specified revenue-use design.
evidence_quote: Sharing out funds per capita globally also appealed in Australia (59%), India (85%), South Africa (53%) and the United Kingdom (56%), but not in the United States (44%).
notes: Heterogeneity across countries drives mixed direction.

title: How to win public support for a global carbon tax
authors: Stefano Carattini; Steffen Kallbekken; Anton Orlov
geographic_scope: Australia, India, South Africa, United Kingdom, United States (survey); global revenue-use designs
raw_determinant: could succeed if its revenues were allocated to all countries. Transferring wealth from developed to only emerging countries might trigger opposition in the former
standardized_candidate_label: Allocate climate-fund revenues to all countries vs only emerging/developing
definition: For a pooled/global carbon tax, allocating mitigation funds to all countries versus allocating them only to emerging/developing countries.
mechanism: Perceived fairness depends on whether developed countries also receive benefits; excluding them can trigger opposition among expected net payers.
exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical global carbon tax with revenues allocated to mitigation projects in all countries versus only the developing/emerging world.
outcome_variable: Support for a global carbon tax with the specified international allocation rule.
evidence_quote: Thus, an international climate fund could succeed if its revenues were allocated to all countries. Transferring wealth from developed to only emerging countries might trigger opposition in the former.
notes: Inferred from the experiment’s cross-country support patterns; no exact % shown in the text.

title: How to win public support for a global carbon tax
authors: Stefano Carattini; Steffen Kallbekken; Anton Orlov
geographic_scope: Australia, India, South Africa, United Kingdom, United States (survey); global revenue-use designs
raw_determinant: A global system of harmonized carbon taxes, in which countries retain control over the revenues
standardized_candidate_label: Revenue control via harmonized carbon taxes (countries retain control over revenue use)
definition: Harmonized carbon taxes where countries retain control over how revenues are used.
mechanism: National revenue control can reduce sovereignty concerns and improve acceptability in many countries, but support is not uniform (South Africans preferred international dividend sharing).
exact_tax_policy_context: Recommendation/conclusion from the survey framing: harmonized carbon taxes with countries keeping revenue control, contrasted with pooled global revenue redistribution.
outcome_variable: Perceived feasibility/acceptability of implementing carbon taxes under different revenue-governance designs.
evidence_quote: The most feasible option? A global system of harmonized carbon taxes, in which countries retain control over the revenues.
statistical_or_result_detail: No % given for this governance framing; described as 'most feasible' with support reported as country-dependent.
notes: Support is stronger in AU/IN/UK/US; South Africa preferred revenue sharing as a dividend.

title: How to win public support for a global carbon tax
authors: Stefano Carattini; Steffen Kallbekken; Anton Orlov
geographic_scope: Australia, India, South Africa, United Kingdom, United States (survey); global revenue-use designs
raw_determinant: South Africans preferred the revenues to be shared as a dividend, because this would do more to relieve the economic burden of a carbon tax there
standardized_candidate_label: Economic burden exposure drives preference for dividend recycling (South Africa)
definition: In South Africa, dividend-style revenue recycling is preferred because it would relieve the economic burden of the carbon tax.
mechanism: Expected net costs make direct per-capita compensation more attractive than alternative revenue uses.
exact_tax_policy_context: Survey results for South Africa comparing revenue uses (including dividend options) under a hypothetical global carbon tax.
outcome_variable: Policy preference among carbon-tax revenue-use designs.
evidence_quote: South Africans preferred the revenues to be shared as a dividend, because this would do more to relieve the economic burden of a carbon tax there.
statistical_or_result_detail: South Africa: per-capita global dividend support 53% (context for dividend preference).
notes: Mechanism explicitly tied to reducing the economic burden.

title: How to win public support for a global carbon tax
authors: Stefano Carattini; Steffen Kallbekken; Anton Orlov
geographic_scope: Australia, India, South Africa, United Kingdom, United States (survey); global revenue-use designs
raw_determinant: calls to ‘axe the tax’ faded once residents received rebates on income tax
standardized_candidate_label: Revenue rebates/dividends reduce opposition after implementation (British Columbia example)
definition: Carbon-tax opposition can dissipate when citizens receive income-tax rebates/revenue returns after implementation.
mechanism: Visible compensation improves perceived net effects and reduces incentives for anti-tax mobilization.
exact_tax_policy_context: British Columbia carbon tax (2008): support changes described after income-tax rebates.
outcome_variable: Public opposition/acceptability of the carbon tax over time.
evidence_quote: calls to ‘axe the tax’ faded once residents received rebates on income tax
exclusion_reason_if_not_main: Historical/illustrative evidence; no experimental estimate in the paper’s own survey.

title: How to win public support for a global carbon tax
authors: Stefano Carattini; Steffen Kallbekken; Anton Orlov
geographic_scope: Australia, India, South Africa, United Kingdom, United States (survey); global revenue-use designs
raw_determinant: Macron’s fuel tax is seen as unfair ... revenues are not given back to citizens ... hits low-income and rural households hardest
standardized_candidate_label: Perceived unfairness from sharp fuel-price rises and no revenue recycling reduces support
definition: Unfairly perceived fuel taxes—sharp price hikes and no revenue returned—reduce acceptability, especially when burdens fall on low-income/rural households.
mechanism: Lack of compensating revenue recycling and regressive burden perceptions increase opposition.
exact_tax_policy_context: France: Macron’s fuel/petrol tax described as unfair due to sharp price rises and no revenue rebates.
outcome_variable: Public opposition to the fuel tax (attitude/acceptability).
evidence_quote: Macron’s fuel tax is seen as unfair. Price rises are sharp and the revenues are not given back to citizens, so the French petrol levy hits low-income and rural households hardest.
exclusion_reason_if_not_main: Contextual claim; not tested quantitatively in the paper’s survey experiment.

title: How to win public support for a global carbon tax
authors: Stefano Carattini; Steffen Kallbekken; Anton Orlov
geographic_scope: Australia, India, South Africa, United Kingdom, United States (survey); global revenue-use designs
raw_determinant: Most people underestimate the benefits of lower emissions and overestimate drawbacks such as job losses
standardized_candidate_label: Misperceived costs/benefits (underestimate gains, overestimate job losses) reduce support
definition: Misinformed beliefs that emissions benefits are small and job losses are large reduce support for carbon taxes.
mechanism: Belief errors distort the perceived balance of costs and benefits, lowering acceptance.
exact_tax_policy_context: General public-attitude patterns toward carbon taxes discussed in the paper.
outcome_variable: Attitudes toward carbon-tax acceptability.
evidence_quote: Most people underestimate the benefits of lower emissions and overestimate drawbacks such as job losses.
exclusion_reason_if_not_main: Not measured in this paper’s survey experiment; presented as a general pattern from prior literature.

title: How to win public support for a global carbon tax
authors: Stefano Carattini; Steffen Kallbekken; Anton Orlov
geographic_scope: Australia, India, South Africa, United Kingdom, United States (survey); global revenue-use designs
raw_determinant: Acceptance increases once the policy is enacted, so implementing a tax gradually is more successful
definition: Acceptance increases after enactment; gradual implementation of carbon taxes is more successful.
mechanism: Learning reduces uncertainty and lets citizens incorporate realized impacts and any compensation.
exact_tax_policy_context: General discussion of how carbon-tax acceptance evolves after enactment.
outcome_variable: Public acceptance/acceptability of a carbon tax.
evidence_quote: Acceptance increases once the policy is enacted, so implementing a tax gradually is more successful.
exclusion_reason_if_not_main: Presented as general pattern; no gradual-implementation treatment is estimated in this paper’s survey.

title: How to win public support for a global carbon tax
authors: Stefano Carattini; Steffen Kallbekken; Anton Orlov
geographic_scope: Australia, India, South Africa, United Kingdom, United States (survey); global revenue-use designs
raw_determinant: people tend to like ‘progressive’ policies under which costs are borne mainly by those most able to pay
standardized_candidate_label: Preference for progressive burden-sharing increases acceptability
definition: Carbon-tax support is higher when the policy is perceived as progressive and costs fall mainly on those most able to pay.
mechanism: Vertical equity improves perceived fairness relative to regressive cost shifting.
exact_tax_policy_context: General discussion of preferences for progressive carbon-tax design.
outcome_variable: Attitudes/policy preference toward carbon taxes.
evidence_quote: people tend to like ‘progressive’ policies under which costs are borne mainly by those most able to pay.
exclusion_reason_if_not_main: No direct manipulation/estimate of progressivity in this paper’s survey results (as presented here).

title: How to win public support for a global carbon tax
authors: Stefano Carattini; Steffen Kallbekken; Anton Orlov
geographic_scope: Australia, India, South Africa, United Kingdom, United States (survey); global revenue-use designs
raw_determinant: carbon taxes do not swell government coffers, which appeals to the political right
standardized_candidate_label: Political-right appeal when revenues are returned (no swelling government coffers)
definition: Returning carbon-tax revenue to citizens (instead of keeping it in government coffers) appeals to the political right.
mechanism: Fits right-leaning preferences against expanding government spending, improving attitudes to the tax.
exact_tax_policy_context: Climate dividends mechanism described in the paper’s 'PEOPLE’S PAYOUT' box.
outcome_variable: Attitudes toward carbon taxes with full revenue recycling.
evidence_quote: If all the money is given back to citizens, carbon taxes do not swell government coffers, which appeals to the political right.
exclusion_reason_if_not_main: Ideological mechanism explained; not empirically estimated in this paper’s survey.

title: How to win public support for a global carbon tax
authors: Stefano Carattini; Steffen Kallbekken; Anton Orlov
geographic_scope: Australia, India, South Africa, United Kingdom, United States (survey); global revenue-use designs
raw_determinant: The left is also interested because the average tax burden is unchanged and low-income households are better off
standardized_candidate_label: Political-left appeal when dividends keep average burden neutral and help low-income
definition: Carbon taxes with dividends that keep average burden unchanged and improve low-income outcomes appeal to the political left.
mechanism: Aligns with left-leaning redistributive goals by protecting low-income households.
exact_tax_policy_context: Climate dividends mechanism described in the paper’s 'PEOPLE’S PAYOUT' box.
outcome_variable: Attitudes toward carbon taxes with dividends.
evidence_quote: The left is also interested because the average tax burden is unchanged and low-income households are better off.
exclusion_reason_if_not_main: Ideological mechanism explained; no direct left/right treatment or estimate in the survey results as reported here.

title: The public’s views on climate policies in seven large global south countries
authors: Richard T. Carson; Jiajun Lu; Emily A. Khossravi; Gunnar Köhlin; Erik Sterner; Thomas Sterner; Dale Whittington
geographic_scope: Chile, Colombia, India, Kenya, Nigeria, South Africa, Vietnam
standardized_candidate_label: Earmark carbon tax revenue to education and health
definition: Using carbon tax revenue to fund education and health programmes (instead of returning it equally to all citizens).
mechanism: Earmarking to highly valued public services likely increases perceived fairness and visible benefits, raising carbon tax revenue acceptability.
exact_tax_policy_context: Best–worst ranking of uses for carbon tax revenue; options compared to 'Return in equal shares' as the reference.
outcome_variable: Ranked preference (1=most preferred) for carbon tax revenue earmarks.
evidence_quote: Table 6: 'Spend on education and health'—Combined rank 1; coefficient 1.195*** vs 'Return in equal shares'.
statistical_or_result_detail: Combined rank=1; coefficient vs equal-shares ref=1.195***.
notes: Highest in all countries except Kenya (rank 2).

title: The public’s views on climate policies in seven large global south countries
authors: Richard T. Carson; Jiajun Lu; Emily A. Khossravi; Gunnar Köhlin; Erik Sterner; Thomas Sterner; Dale Whittington
geographic_scope: Chile, Colombia, India, Kenya, Nigeria, South Africa, Vietnam
standardized_candidate_label: Earmark carbon tax revenue to clean R&D
definition: Invest carbon tax revenue in clean energy research and development to reduce future technology costs.
mechanism: Positions the tax as funding solutions and innovation for future decarbonization, supporting perceived effectiveness and legitimacy.
exact_tax_policy_context: Best–worst ranking of uses for carbon tax revenue; relative to 'Return in equal shares' reference.
outcome_variable: Ranked preference (1=most preferred) for carbon tax revenue earmarks.
evidence_quote: Table 6: 'Invest in clean R&D'—Combined rank 3; coefficient 0.916*** vs 'Return in equal shares'.
statistical_or_result_detail: Combined rank=3; coefficient=0.916***.
notes: Consistently among the top revenue-use options.

title: The public’s views on climate policies in seven large global south countries
authors: Richard T. Carson; Jiajun Lu; Emily A. Khossravi; Gunnar Köhlin; Erik Sterner; Thomas Sterner; Dale Whittington
geographic_scope: Chile, Colombia, India, Kenya, Nigeria, South Africa, Vietnam
standardized_candidate_label: Earmark carbon tax revenue to solar panel subsidies
definition: Use carbon tax revenue to subsidize solar panel installations.
mechanism: Provides tangible renewable-energy support, likely increasing perceived usefulness and immediate benefits from the carbon tax.
exact_tax_policy_context: Best–worst ranking of uses for carbon tax revenue; relative to 'Return in equal shares' reference.
outcome_variable: Ranked preference (1=most preferred) for carbon tax revenue earmarks.
evidence_quote: Table 6: 'Subsidize solar panels'—Combined rank 2; coefficient 0.956*** vs 'Return in equal shares'.
statistical_or_result_detail: Combined rank=2; coefficient=0.956***.
notes: Kenya ranks solar highest (rank 1).

title: The public’s views on climate policies in seven large global south countries
authors: Richard T. Carson; Jiajun Lu; Emily A. Khossravi; Gunnar Köhlin; Erik Sterner; Thomas Sterner; Dale Whittington
geographic_scope: Chile, Colombia, India, Kenya, Nigeria, South Africa, Vietnam
standardized_candidate_label: Targeted rebates of carbon tax revenue to the poor
definition: Return carbon tax revenue specifically to poor households (targeted compensation).
mechanism: Addresses distributional concerns by compensating those seen as more vulnerable, increasing perceived fairness of the tax.
exact_tax_policy_context: Best–worst ranking of uses for carbon tax revenue; compared to 'Return in equal shares' reference.
outcome_variable: Ranked preference (1=most preferred) for carbon tax revenue earmarks.
evidence_quote: Table 6: 'Return to poor'—Combined rank 5; coefficient 0.276*** vs 'Return in equal shares'.
statistical_or_result_detail: Combined rank=5; coefficient=0.276*** (Vietnam coefficient 0.054, no star).
notes: More preferred than uniform equal-share rebates on average.

title: The public’s views on climate policies in seven large global south countries
authors: Richard T. Carson; Jiajun Lu; Emily A. Khossravi; Gunnar Köhlin; Erik Sterner; Thomas Sterner; Dale Whittington
geographic_scope: Chile, Colombia, India, Kenya, Nigeria, South Africa, Vietnam
standardized_candidate_label: Uniform equal-share dividend of carbon tax revenue
definition: Return carbon tax revenue equally to all citizens (uniform rebates/dividend).
mechanism: Less targeted compensation may be perceived as less fair or less aligned with priority uses, reducing preference relative to earmarking.
exact_tax_policy_context: Best–worst ranking of uses for carbon tax revenue; treated as the base reference option.
outcome_variable: Ranked preference (1=most preferred) for carbon tax revenue earmarks.
evidence_quote: Table 6: 'Return in equal shares' is the base reference; Combined rank 7 (lowest).
statistical_or_result_detail: Combined rank=7; coefficient normalized to 0.000.
notes: Lowest-ranked overall use of carbon tax revenue.

title: The public’s views on climate policies in seven large global south countries
authors: Richard T. Carson; Jiajun Lu; Emily A. Khossravi; Gunnar Köhlin; Erik Sterner; Thomas Sterner; Dale Whittington
geographic_scope: Chile, Colombia, India, Kenya, Nigeria, South Africa, Vietnam
standardized_candidate_label: Use carbon tax revenue to reduce the government deficit
definition: Apply carbon tax revenue to reduce the government budget deficit.
mechanism: Often seen as less directly beneficial (lower perceived value) but may be viewed more positively where fiscal/financial priorities dominate.
exact_tax_policy_context: Best–worst ranking of uses for carbon tax revenue; compared to 'Return in equal shares' reference across countries.
outcome_variable: Ranked preference (1=most preferred) for carbon tax revenue earmarks.
evidence_quote: Table 6: 'Reduced government deficit'—Combined rank 6; coefficient 0.027 (no overall significance marker).
statistical_or_result_detail: Combined rank=6; coefficient=0.027; Kenya rank 5 (0.609***), Chile rank 7 (−0.241***).
notes: Country heterogeneity: negative in Chile/South Africa/Vietnam; positive in Kenya/Nigeria.

title: The public’s views on climate policies in seven large global south countries
authors: Richard T. Carson; Jiajun Lu; Emily A. Khossravi; Gunnar Köhlin; Erik Sterner; Thomas Sterner; Dale Whittington
geographic_scope: Chile, Colombia, India, Kenya, Nigeria, South Africa, Vietnam
standardized_candidate_label: Earmark carbon tax revenue to road investment
definition: Invest carbon tax revenue in roads.
mechanism: A visible earmark for local infrastructure may increase perceived legitimacy versus cash or deficit uses, though it is not the top-ranked option.
exact_tax_policy_context: Best–worst ranking of uses for carbon tax revenue; compared to 'Return in equal shares' reference.
outcome_variable: Ranked preference (1=most preferred) for carbon tax revenue earmarks.
evidence_quote: Table 6: 'Invest in roads'—Combined rank 4; coefficient 0.542*** vs 'Return in equal shares'.
statistical_or_result_detail: Combined rank=4; coefficient=0.542***.
notes: Ranked above equal-share dividends across countries.

title: Fighting Climate Change: International Attitudes toward Climate Policies
authors: Antoine Dechezleprêtre, Adrien Fabre, Tobias Kruse, Bluebery Planterose, Ana Sanchez Chico, Stefanie Stantcheva
geographic_scope: 20 countries: Australia, Brazil, Canada, China, Denmark, France, Germany, India, Indonesia, Italy, Japan, Mexico, Poland, South Africa, South Korea, Spain, Turkey, Ukraine, United Kingdom, United States
raw_determinant: Believes the policy would reduce GHG emissions
definition: Belief that the tax will lower greenhouse-gas emissions.
mechanism: Expected abatement makes the tax seem worthwhile and increases support.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for carbon tax with cash transfers and related tax outcomes (Section IV.C; Figure 14).
outcome_variable: Support for carbon tax with cash transfers
evidence_quote: support for a given climate policy is strongly predicted by ... that the policy is helpful in reducing emissions
statistical_or_result_detail: Explains 14% of support variance.

title: Fighting Climate Change: International Attitudes toward Climate Policies
authors: Antoine Dechezleprêtre, Adrien Fabre, Tobias Kruse, Bluebery Planterose, Ana Sanchez Chico, Stefanie Stantcheva
geographic_scope: 20 countries: Australia, Brazil, Canada, China, Denmark, France, Germany, India, Indonesia, Italy, Japan, Mexico, Poland, South Africa, South Korea, Spain, Turkey, Ukraine, United Kingdom, United States
raw_determinant: Believes the policy would reduce air pollution
definition: Belief that the tax will lower air pollution.
mechanism: Local co-benefits increase the policy's perceived value and support.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for carbon tax with cash transfers and related tax outcomes (Section IV.C; Figure 14).
outcome_variable: Support for carbon tax with cash transfers
evidence_quote: support for a given climate policy is strongly predicted by ... that the policy is helpful in reducing emissions
statistical_or_result_detail: Explains 10.1% of support variance.

title: Fighting Climate Change: International Attitudes toward Climate Policies
authors: Antoine Dechezleprêtre, Adrien Fabre, Tobias Kruse, Bluebery Planterose, Ana Sanchez Chico, Stefanie Stantcheva
geographic_scope: 20 countries: Australia, Brazil, Canada, China, Denmark, France, Germany, India, Indonesia, Italy, Japan, Mexico, Poland, South Africa, South Korea, Spain, Turkey, Ukraine, United Kingdom, United States
raw_determinant: Believes the policy would have positive econ. effects
definition: Belief that the tax will benefit the economy or employment.
mechanism: Lower expected economic harm makes the tax more acceptable.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for carbon tax with cash transfers and related tax outcomes (Section IV.C; Figure 14).
outcome_variable: Support for carbon tax with cash transfers
evidence_quote: Believing that a policy will positively impact the economy is associated with slightly higher policy support
statistical_or_result_detail: Explains 4.4% of support variance.
notes: Weak predictor compared with emissions and self-interest.

title: Fighting Climate Change: International Attitudes toward Climate Policies
authors: Antoine Dechezleprêtre, Adrien Fabre, Tobias Kruse, Bluebery Planterose, Ana Sanchez Chico, Stefanie Stantcheva
geographic_scope: 20 countries: Australia, Brazil, Canada, China, Denmark, France, Germany, India, Indonesia, Italy, Japan, Mexico, Poland, South Africa, South Korea, Spain, Turkey, Ukraine, United Kingdom, United States
definition: Belief that poorer households would be net losers from the tax.
mechanism: Expected regressivity reduces perceived fairness and support.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for carbon tax with cash transfers and related tax outcomes (Section IV.C; Figure 14).
outcome_variable: Support for carbon tax with cash transfers
evidence_quote: Respondents who believe that low-income earners will lose are less supportive of the policy
statistical_or_result_detail: Explains 8.3% of support variance.

title: Fighting Climate Change: International Attitudes toward Climate Policies
authors: Antoine Dechezleprêtre, Adrien Fabre, Tobias Kruse, Bluebery Planterose, Ana Sanchez Chico, Stefanie Stantcheva
geographic_scope: 20 countries: Australia, Brazil, Canada, China, Denmark, France, Germany, India, Indonesia, Italy, Japan, Mexico, Poland, South Africa, South Korea, Spain, Turkey, Ukraine, United Kingdom, United States
standardized_candidate_label: Perceived private cost to own household
definition: Belief that the tax will financially hurt one's own household.
mechanism: Expected private costs lower willingness to support the tax.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for carbon tax with cash transfers and related tax outcomes (Section IV.C; Figure 14).
outcome_variable: Support for carbon tax with cash transfers
evidence_quote: Those who think they will themselves lose from a given policy are much less likely to support it
statistical_or_result_detail: Explains 14.5% of support variance.

title: Fighting Climate Change: International Attitudes toward Climate Policies
authors: Antoine Dechezleprêtre, Adrien Fabre, Tobias Kruse, Bluebery Planterose, Ana Sanchez Chico, Stefanie Stantcheva
geographic_scope: 20 countries: Australia, Brazil, Canada, China, Denmark, France, Germany, India, Indonesia, Italy, Japan, Mexico, Poland, South Africa, South Korea, Spain, Turkey, Ukraine, United Kingdom, United States
definition: Belief that the tax design is fair.
mechanism: Fairness perceptions reduce opposition and raise acceptability.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for carbon tax with cash transfers and related tax outcomes (Section IV.C; Figure 14).
outcome_variable: Support for carbon tax with cash transfers
evidence_quote: there is a close connection between the respondent believing that a policy is fair and supporting it
statistical_or_result_detail: Raw correlation with support = 0.89.

title: Fighting Climate Change: International Attitudes toward Climate Policies
authors: Antoine Dechezleprêtre, Adrien Fabre, Tobias Kruse, Bluebery Planterose, Ana Sanchez Chico, Stefanie Stantcheva
geographic_scope: 20 countries: Australia, Brazil, Canada, China, Denmark, France, Germany, India, Indonesia, Italy, Japan, Mexico, Poland, South Africa, South Korea, Spain, Turkey, Ukraine, United Kingdom, United States
raw_determinant: cash transfers to the poorest households / cash transfers to constrained households

mechanism: Targeted compensation makes the tax more progressive and easier to accept.

outcome_variable: Support for carbon tax variants
evidence_quote: majority support for carbon taxes with transfers to the poorest or the most constrained households
statistical_or_result_detail: Preferred over equal cash transfers.

title: Fighting Climate Change: International Attitudes toward Climate Policies
authors: Antoine Dechezleprêtre, Adrien Fabre, Tobias Kruse, Bluebery Planterose, Ana Sanchez Chico, Stefanie Stantcheva
geographic_scope: 20 countries: Australia, Brazil, Canada, China, Denmark, France, Germany, India, Indonesia, Italy, Japan, Mexico, Poland, South Africa, South Korea, Spain, Turkey, Ukraine, United Kingdom, United States
raw_determinant: fund environmental infrastructures, subsidize low-carbon technologies, or reduce income taxes

definition: Carbon-tax revenue is earmarked for green investment, low-carbon subsidies, or personal income-tax cuts.
mechanism: Seen as effective and more acceptable than lump-sum rebates.

evidence_quote: Carbon taxes with revenues used to fund environmental infrastructures, subsidize low-carbon technologies, or reduce income taxes benefit from around 70 percent higher support

title: Fighting Climate Change: International Attitudes toward Climate Policies
authors: Antoine Dechezleprêtre, Adrien Fabre, Tobias Kruse, Bluebery Planterose, Ana Sanchez Chico, Stefanie Stantcheva
geographic_scope: 20 countries: Australia, Brazil, Canada, China, Denmark, France, Germany, India, Indonesia, Italy, Japan, Mexico, Poland, South Africa, South Korea, Spain, Turkey, Ukraine, United Kingdom, United States
raw_determinant: equal cash transfers to all households
definition: Carbon-tax revenue is returned equally to all households.
mechanism: Less progressive than targeted or green recycling, lowering support.

evidence_quote: Carbon taxes with revenues used to fund equal transfers to everyone only generate 37 percent support in high-income countries
statistical_or_result_detail: 37% support in high-income countries; 59% in middle-income countries.

title: Fighting Climate Change: International Attitudes toward Climate Policies
authors: Antoine Dechezleprêtre, Adrien Fabre, Tobias Kruse, Bluebery Planterose, Ana Sanchez Chico, Stefanie Stantcheva
geographic_scope: 20 countries: Australia, Brazil, Canada, China, Denmark, France, Germany, India, Indonesia, Italy, Japan, Mexico, Poland, South Africa, South Korea, Spain, Turkey, Ukraine, United Kingdom, United States

title: Fighting Climate Change: International Attitudes toward Climate Policies
authors: Antoine Dechezleprêtre, Adrien Fabre, Tobias Kruse, Bluebery Planterose, Ana Sanchez Chico, Stefanie Stantcheva
geographic_scope: 20 countries: Australia, Brazil, Canada, China, Denmark, France, Germany, India, Indonesia, Italy, Japan, Mexico, Poland, South Africa, South Korea, Spain, Turkey, Ukraine, United Kingdom, United States

title: Fighting Climate Change: International Attitudes toward Climate Policies
authors: Antoine Dechezleprêtre, Adrien Fabre, Tobias Kruse, Bluebery Planterose, Ana Sanchez Chico, Stefanie Stantcheva
geographic_scope: 20 countries: Australia, Brazil, Canada, China, Denmark, France, Germany, India, Indonesia, Italy, Japan, Mexico, Poland, South Africa, South Korea, Spain, Turkey, Ukraine, United Kingdom, United States

evidence_quote: The Climate policies treatment ... significantly increase support
