title: Fighting Climate Change: International Attitudes toward Climate Policies
authors: Antoine Dechezleprêtre, Adrien Fabre, Tobias Kruse, Bluebery Planterose, Ana Sanchez Chico, Stefanie Stantcheva
geographic_scope: 20 countries: Australia, Brazil, Canada, China, Denmark, France, Germany, India, Indonesia, Italy, Japan, Mexico, Poland, South Africa, South Korea, Spain, Turkey, Ukraine, United Kingdom, United States
definition: Exposure to both the climate-impacts and climate-policies videos.
mechanism: Combines concern about climate harms with explanation of policy design.
exact_tax_policy_context: Section V.B; Figure 16.
outcome_variable: Support for carbon tax with cash transfers
evidence_quote: The combination of the Climate impacts and Climate policies treatments have even stronger effects
statistical_or_result_detail: About 33% of the control-group mean; strongest effects.

title: Fighting Climate Change: International Attitudes toward Climate Policies
authors: Antoine Dechezleprêtre, Adrien Fabre, Tobias Kruse, Bluebery Planterose, Ana Sanchez Chico, Stefanie Stantcheva
geographic_scope: 20 countries: Australia, Brazil, Canada, China, Denmark, France, Germany, India, Indonesia, Italy, Japan, Mexico, Poland, South Africa, South Korea, Spain, Turkey, Ukraine, United Kingdom, United States
definition: Video on local climate-change impacts.
mechanism: Raises concern and knowledge more than tax support.
exact_tax_policy_context: Section V.B; Figure 16.
outcome_variable: Support for carbon tax with cash transfers
evidence_quote: The Climate impacts treatment has the smallest effects on support
statistical_or_result_detail: Did not substantially change public-policy support.
notes: Stronger for private behaviors than for tax support.

title: Fighting Climate Change: International Attitudes toward Climate Policies
authors: Antoine Dechezleprêtre, Adrien Fabre, Tobias Kruse, Bluebery Planterose, Ana Sanchez Chico, Stefanie Stantcheva
geographic_scope: 20 countries: Australia, Brazil, Canada, China, Denmark, France, Germany, India, Indonesia, Italy, Japan, Mexico, Poland, South Africa, South Korea, Spain, Turkey, Ukraine, United Kingdom, United States
raw_determinant: Believes the high-income earners will lose
definition: Belief that richer households would be net losers from the tax.
mechanism: Signals progressivity; association with support varies by country.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for carbon tax variants; Section IV.C and Supplemental Appendix Tables A13–A14.
outcome_variable: Support for carbon tax with cash transfers
evidence_quote: in a few countries ... the belief that the high-income earners will lose is even positively associated with support
page_or_section: Section IV.C; Supplemental Appendix Tables A13–A14
statistical_or_result_detail: Positive in Canada, France, India, Japan, Mexico, Spain, Turkey, and Ukraine.

title: French Attitudes on Climate Change, Carbon Taxation and other Climate Policies
authors: Thomas Douenne; Adrien Fabre
raw_determinant: overestimation of its impact on one’s purchasing power
standardized_candidate_label: Misperceived (overestimated) purchasing power losses from the carbon tax
definition: Belief that the Tax & Dividend would reduce one’s household purchasing power (even when expected exposure is low).
mechanism: Perceived personal net losses make the carbon tax seem unfair and not worth the cost, reducing acceptance.
exact_tax_policy_context: Tax & Dividend: +50€/tCO2 carbon tax increase; extra revenue redistributed lump-sum to all adults.
outcome_variable: Approval of Tax & Dividend (carbon tax acceptance)
evidence_quote: erroneous perceptions ... overestimation of its impact on one’s purchasing power. For instance, 30% ... believe their household would lose
statistical_or_result_detail: 30% of people using neither gas nor domestic fuel still think their household would lose purchasing power.
notes: Presented as an explanation for rejection; no direct regression coefficient shown in this excerpt.

title: French Attitudes on Climate Change, Carbon Taxation and other Climate Policies
authors: Thomas Douenne; Adrien Fabre
raw_determinant: without mentioning the impact on prices
standardized_candidate_label: Low cost salience (no price-impacts mentioned) increases carbon tax approval
definition: Approval is higher when the survey omits the carbon tax’s price-increase impacts.
mechanism: Less salience of immediate price costs increases perceived net benefits, raising approval.
exact_tax_policy_context: Tax & Dividend: revenue returned to all households; alternative wording either omits or mentions the impact on prices.
outcome_variable: Approval of Tax & Dividend (carbon tax acceptance)
evidence_quote: without mentioning the impact on prices ... higher approval rate of 37%
statistical_or_result_detail: Approval rises to 37% when price impacts are not mentioned (vs 10% in the main framing).
notes: Within-questionnaire framing experiment; cost-salience vs benefits-only wording.

title: French Attitudes on Climate Change, Carbon Taxation and other Climate Policies
authors: Thomas Douenne; Adrien Fabre
standardized_candidate_label: Perceived rural distributional harm from the carbon tax
definition: Belief that Tax & Dividend would penalize rural households.
mechanism: Perceived geographic unfairness lowers willingness to accept the carbon tax increase.
exact_tax_policy_context: Tax & Dividend (+50€/tCO2) described to respondents with sectoral price impacts salient.
outcome_variable: Approval of Tax & Dividend (carbon tax acceptance)
evidence_quote: the tax would penalize rural households (47%)
statistical_or_result_detail: 47% cite rural penalties as a main problem with Tax & Dividend.

title: French Attitudes on Climate Change, Carbon Taxation and other Climate Policies
authors: Thomas Douenne; Adrien Fabre
standardized_candidate_label: Suspicion that the carbon tax is a pretext to raise taxes
definition: Belief that the carbon tax policy is mainly intended to raise taxes rather than reduce pollution.
mechanism: Low trust in tax motives increases perceived unfairness and reduces acceptance.
exact_tax_policy_context: Tax & Dividend (+50€/tCO2) and its perceived problems.
outcome_variable: Approval of Tax & Dividend (carbon tax acceptance)
evidence_quote: policy is simply a pretext to increase taxes (43%)
statistical_or_result_detail: 43% cite 'pretext for raising taxes' as a main problem.

title: French Attitudes on Climate Change, Carbon Taxation and other Climate Policies
authors: Thomas Douenne; Adrien Fabre
standardized_candidate_label: Belief that the carbon tax is ineffective
definition: Belief that Tax & Dividend would not effectively reduce pollution.
mechanism: Low perceived effectiveness makes the policy seem not worth the costs, reducing acceptance.
exact_tax_policy_context: Tax & Dividend (+50€/tCO2) described to respondents.
outcome_variable: Approval of Tax & Dividend (carbon tax acceptance)
evidence_quote: ineffective to reduce pollution (37%)
statistical_or_result_detail: 37% list ineffectiveness for pollution reduction as a problem.

title: French Attitudes on Climate Change, Carbon Taxation and other Climate Policies
authors: Thomas Douenne; Adrien Fabre
raw_determinant: alternatives are insufficient or too expensive
standardized_candidate_label: Perceived lack of affordable alternatives to fossil fuels
definition: Belief that households cannot substitute away from fossil fuels because alternatives are insufficient or too expensive.
mechanism: If substitution seems infeasible, people expect low emissions reduction and view the tax as unfair, reducing acceptance.
exact_tax_policy_context: Tax & Dividend (+50€/tCO2) and perceived barriers.
outcome_variable: Approval of Tax & Dividend (carbon tax acceptance)
evidence_quote: perceived lack of alternatives, seen as insufficient or too expensive (31%)
statistical_or_result_detail: 31% cite lack of alternatives (insufficient/too expensive) as a key problem.

title: French Attitudes on Climate Change, Carbon Taxation and other Climate Policies
authors: Thomas Douenne; Adrien Fabre
standardized_candidate_label: Earmarking carbon tax revenue to green/energy-transition investments
definition: Carbon tax approval is higher when its revenue finances energy-transition and green investments (earmarking).
mechanism: Green earmarking signals effectiveness and funds alternatives, increasing perceived net benefits and credibility of carbon pricing.
exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon tax (+50€/tCO2) with revenue recycled to different uses (revenue recycling acceptance question).
outcome_variable: Approval of carbon tax by revenue-recycling use
evidence_quote: Overall, the preferred revenue recyclings are investments in the energy transition.
notes: Supported by discussion that people see carbon taxation as effective only when financing green investments.

title: French Attitudes on Climate Change, Carbon Taxation and other Climate Policies
authors: Thomas Douenne; Adrien Fabre
raw_determinant: targeted ... constrained with respect to their consumption of petroleum products (fifth with 55% approval)
standardized_candidate_label: Targeted transfer for petroleum-constrained households increases acceptance
definition: Using carbon tax revenue for a lump-sum transfer targeted to households constrained in petroleum consumption is more acceptable than flat transfers.
mechanism: Compensation aligns with perceived exposure and facilitates substitution away from petroleum-intensive consumption.
exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon tax revenue recycling options including targeted lump-sum compensation to constrained households.
outcome_variable: Approval of carbon tax revenue recycling to transfers
evidence_quote: preferred “lump-sum” transfer ... targeted to people constrained with respect to their consumption of petroleum products (fifth with 55% approval).
statistical_or_result_detail: 55% approval.

title: French Attitudes on Climate Change, Carbon Taxation and other Climate Policies
authors: Thomas Douenne; Adrien Fabre
raw_determinant: reduction in the VAT rate (61% approval)
standardized_candidate_label: Use carbon tax revenue to reduce VAT increases acceptance
definition: Carbon tax revenue used to reduce the VAT rate leads to higher acceptance.
mechanism: Perceived purchasing-power gains and improved fairness (offsetting regressive VAT) raise support.
exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon tax revenue recycling options including VAT reduction.
outcome_variable: Approval of carbon tax revenue recycling
evidence_quote: second preferred mechanism for revenue recycling is a reduction in the VAT rate (61% approval).
statistical_or_result_detail: 61% approval.

title: French Attitudes on Climate Change, Carbon Taxation and other Climate Policies
authors: Thomas Douenne; Adrien Fabre
definition: Higher index of knowledge about climate change (anthropogenic origin, factors, and emissions target).
mechanism: More knowledge is associated with higher concern and greater willingness to support carbon tax policies.
exact_tax_policy_context: Acceptance of Tax & Dividend (+50€/tCO2 carbon tax; revenue returned lump-sum to adults).
outcome_variable: Acceptance of Tax & dividend
evidence_quote: Knowledge about CC 0.048*** (0.009)
statistical_or_result_detail: Coefficient +0.048*** (column 2) for Tax & dividend acceptance.

title: French Attitudes on Climate Change, Carbon Taxation and other Climate Policies
authors: Thomas Douenne; Adrien Fabre
standardized_candidate_label: Perceived catastrophic severity of climate change ('disastrous')
definition: Belief that climate change impacts would be 'disastrous' (lifestyles largely altered).
mechanism: Greater perceived severity increases perceived urgency and support for carbon taxation.
exact_tax_policy_context: Acceptance of Tax & Dividend (+50€/tCO2 carbon tax).
outcome_variable: Acceptance of Tax & dividend
evidence_quote: CC is disastrous 0.037*** (0.018)
statistical_or_result_detail: Positive and significant in column (2); coefficient 0.037***.
notes: Not significant in column (1) but significant in column (2).

title: French Attitudes on Climate Change, Carbon Taxation and other Climate Policies
authors: Thomas Douenne; Adrien Fabre
definition: Respondent identifies as an ecologist (political identity in the survey).
mechanism: Value alignment with environmental priorities increases willingness to accept carbon taxes.
exact_tax_policy_context: Acceptance of Tax & Dividend (+50€/tCO2 carbon tax).
outcome_variable: Acceptance of Tax & dividend
statistical_or_result_detail: Tax & dividend acceptance: +0.126*** (column 1) and +0.082*** (column 2).

title: French Attitudes on Climate Change, Carbon Taxation and other Climate Policies
authors: Thomas Douenne; Adrien Fabre
standardized_candidate_label: Support for the Yellow Vests movement
definition: Respondent reports supporting the Yellow Vests movement.
mechanism: Political alignment with Yellow Vests is associated with lower acceptance of carbon tax increases (likely distrust/objections).
exact_tax_policy_context: Acceptance of Tax & Dividend (+50€/tCO2 carbon tax).
outcome_variable: Acceptance of Tax & dividend
statistical_or_result_detail: Tax & dividend acceptance: −0.222*** (column 1) and −0.048*** (column 2).
notes: Other Yellow Vests modalities (e.g., understands, is part) are also negative in Table II.

title: French Attitudes on Climate Change, Carbon Taxation and other Climate Policies
authors: Thomas Douenne; Adrien Fabre
definition: Respondent is 65 years old or older (relative to 18–24 omitted group).
mechanism: Older respondents are less supportive of the carbon tax increase, holding other factors constant.
exact_tax_policy_context: Acceptance of Tax & Dividend (+50€/tCO2).
outcome_variable: Acceptance of Tax & dividend
notes: Age coefficients are negative for all older brackets in column (2).

title: French Attitudes on Climate Change, Carbon Taxation and other Climate Policies
authors: Thomas Douenne; Adrien Fabre
definition: Respondent is male (relative to female).
mechanism: Men are less favorable toward Tax & Dividend even though they may have higher climate knowledge.
exact_tax_policy_context: Acceptance of Tax & Dividend (+50€/tCO2).
outcome_variable: Acceptance of Tax & dividend

title: French Attitudes on Climate Change, Carbon Taxation and other Climate Policies
authors: Thomas Douenne; Adrien Fabre
standardized_candidate_label: Living in larger towns increases acceptance
definition: Higher town-size category (1–5).
mechanism: Urban residence is linked to higher support for the carbon tax increase (likely better alternatives to car use).
exact_tax_policy_context: Acceptance of Tax & Dividend (+50€/tCO2).
outcome_variable: Acceptance of Tax & dividend
statistical_or_result_detail: +0.033*** (column 2) per unit increase in town size category.

title: French Attitudes on Climate Change, Carbon Taxation and other Climate Policies
authors: Thomas Douenne; Adrien Fabre
standardized_candidate_label: Diesel vehicle ownership increases opposition to diesel tax
definition: Households owning a diesel vehicle.
mechanism: Greater expected cost exposure from a diesel tax increase leads to opposition.
exact_tax_policy_context: Increase diesel tax to catch up gasoline taxation level.
outcome_variable: Approval of a catching-up of the diesel tax
evidence_quote: Among the 57% of households who own a diesel vehicle, the opposition augments to 80%.
statistical_or_result_detail: Opposition is 80% among diesel-vehicle owners (vs 59% overall).

title: French Attitudes on Climate Change, Carbon Taxation and other Climate Policies
authors: Thomas Douenne; Adrien Fabre
raw_determinant: 73% of rural households would be opposed
standardized_candidate_label: Rural residence increases opposition to diesel tax
definition: Rural households vs Paris agglomeration regarding opposition to raising diesel taxes.
mechanism: Rural car dependence and fewer substitutes raise expected burdens from diesel taxation, lowering support.
exact_tax_policy_context: Increase diesel tax to catch up with that of gasoline.
outcome_variable: Approval of a catching-up of the diesel tax
evidence_quote: 73% of rural households would be opposed, vs. only 40% of those living in the Paris agglomeration.
statistical_or_result_detail: Opposition: 73% (rural) vs 40% (Paris agglomeration).

title: French Attitudes on Climate Change, Carbon Taxation and other Climate Policies
authors: Thomas Douenne; Adrien Fabre
raw_determinant: broadly perceived effectiveness ... distributive effect ... richer people fly more
standardized_candidate_label: Perceived effectiveness and progressivity explain support for kerosene tax
definition: Support for a kerosene (aviation) tax is linked to perceived emission-reduction effectiveness and to its distributive effect (richer fly more).
mechanism: If the tax is viewed as effective and relatively progressive, acceptance increases.
exact_tax_policy_context: Tax on kerosene (aviation).
outcome_variable: Approval of a tax on kerosene (aviation)
evidence_quote: main rationale could be ... perceived effectiveness ... and the distributive effect ... since richer people fly more.
statistical_or_result_detail: Overall approval: 70% (“Yes”), including 41% “Yes, completely”.
notes: Rationale is proposed by authors; no direct statistical test of this mechanism is shown in the excerpt.

title: French Attitudes on Climate Change, Carbon Taxation and other Climate Policies
authors: Thomas Douenne; Adrien Fabre
standardized_candidate_label: Higher political interest increases Tax & dividend approval
definition: More interest in politics is associated with greater approval of the Tax & Dividend carbon tax increase.
mechanism: Engagement with politics may increase attention to climate policy objectives and evaluation, raising support.
exact_tax_policy_context: Acceptance of Tax & Dividend (+50€/tCO2) with lump-sum revenue to adults.
outcome_variable: Approval of Tax & dividend
evidence_quote: Interest in politics (0 to 2) 0.034*** (Table II, column 2)
statistical_or_result_detail: 0.034*** (Tax & dividend acceptance, column 2).

title: French Attitudes on Climate Change, Carbon Taxation and other Climate Policies
authors: Thomas Douenne; Adrien Fabre
standardized_candidate_label: More frequent public transit use increases Tax & dividend approval
definition: Higher frequency of public transit is associated with higher approval of the Tax & Dividend.
mechanism: More frequent transit use can provide substitutes for car use, reducing perceived cost exposure from fuel-price increases.
exact_tax_policy_context: Acceptance of Tax & Dividend (+50€/tCO2) carbon tax increase.
outcome_variable: Approval of Tax & dividend
evidence_quote: Frequency of public transit 0.014** (Table II, column 2)
statistical_or_result_detail: 0.014** (Tax & dividend acceptance, column 2).

title: French Attitudes on Climate Change, Carbon Taxation and other Climate Policies
authors: Thomas Douenne; Adrien Fabre
standardized_candidate_label: Belief the carbon tax would penalize the poorest reduces approval
definition: Respondents cite that the Tax & Dividend would penalize the poorest households.
mechanism: Perceived regressive/distributive harm lowers willingness to accept the carbon tax increase.
exact_tax_policy_context: Tax & Dividend (+50€/tCO2) perceived problems; sectoral price impacts salient.
outcome_variable: Approval of Tax & Dividend (carbon tax acceptance)
evidence_quote: threat that the tax could penalize the poorest (sixth position with 29%)
statistical_or_result_detail: 29% cite poorest penalty as a problem.

title: Yellow Vests, Pessimistic Beliefs, and Carbon Tax Aversion
authors: Thomas Douenne; Adrien Fabre
raw_determinant: belief that one does not lose from it (win/lose category)
standardized_candidate_label: Perceived self-interest: household does not lose purchasing power
definition: Respondent believes their household would not lose purchasing power (“win vs lose” self-interest belief) from the carbon tax & dividend reform.
mechanism: Expected personal net benefits reduce perceived costs and increase acceptance/support despite higher energy prices.
exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon tax increase recycled as dividends (uniform 110€/adult and income-targeted variants); causal effect estimated using fuzzy RDD/IV around eligibility and feedback.
outcome_variable: Acceptance of the tax & dividend reform ("Yes" or "Don’t know").
evidence_quote: the belief that one does not lose from it increases the acceptance rate by more than 50 p.p. (53 p.p. in our main specification).
statistical_or_result_detail: IV/fuzzy RDD: coefficient 0.534 => ~+53 p.p. on acceptance (“Yes” or “Don’t know”); Table 5.2.
notes: Estimated with 2SLS: random dividend eligibility (targeted dividend) and discontinuity in win/lose feedback threshold.

title: Yellow Vests, Pessimistic Beliefs, and Carbon Tax Aversion
authors: Thomas Douenne; Adrien Fabre
raw_determinant: Believing that the tax is effective (environmental effectiveness)
standardized_candidate_label: Perceived environmental effectiveness of the carbon tax & dividend
definition: Respondent believes the carbon-tax-and-dividend policy would effectively reduce pollution and combat climate change.
mechanism: Believing the policy will deliver environmental outcomes increases perceived policy legitimacy and willingness to approve.
exact_tax_policy_context: Tax & dividend reform; 2SLS uses random information treatments as instruments for the belief that the policy is environmentally effective.
outcome_variable: Approval of the tax & dividend reform ("Yes").
evidence_quote: Believing that the tax is effective increases the likelihood of approving it by 42 p.p. (1).
statistical_or_result_detail: 2SLS (IV): +0.416 => +42 p.p. on approval when believing effectiveness; Table 5.4.
notes: Environmental effectiveness belief instrumented using random info on scientific consensus and climate change.

title: Yellow Vests, Pessimistic Beliefs, and Carbon Tax Aversion
authors: Thomas Douenne; Adrien Fabre
raw_determinant: Progressivity belief (benefit the poorest / not regressive)
standardized_candidate_label: Perceived progressivity: benefits poorest / not regressive
definition: Respondent believes the tax & dividend is progressive—poorer households gain and the richest (higher energy consumers) lose purchasing power.
mechanism: Perceived distributional fairness and protection of low-income households increase acceptance/support for the reform.
exact_tax_policy_context: Beliefs about progressivity after receiving progressivity information; correlations with acceptance estimated in online Appendix K (no IV identified).
outcome_variable: Support/acceptance after information on progressivity.
evidence_quote: the effect of progressivity remains statistically significant and as high as 27 p.p. in our preferred specification.
statistical_or_result_detail: Non-causal OLS/logit: marginal effect of progressivity belief at mean ≈ +27 p.p. on acceptance after controls.
notes: Estimated with OLS/logit controlling for motives and covariates; interpreted as partial correlation, not causal.

title: Yellow Vests, Pessimistic Beliefs, and Carbon Tax Aversion
authors: Thomas Douenne; Adrien Fabre
raw_determinant: WTP from subjective gain (subjective net purchasing-power change)
standardized_candidate_label: Subjective expected net gain (implied WTP threshold)
definition: Higher subjective expected net gain from the tax & dividend increases acceptance rates and implies a higher willingness to incur losses for support.
mechanism: People support the policy when expected personal gains exceed a loss tolerance, reflecting self-interest.
exact_tax_policy_context: Acceptance/support analyzed as a function of respondents’ subjective gain (euro ranges), used to infer willingness to pay in Figure L.1.
outcome_variable: Implied willingness to pay (median loss tolerated) / acceptance as function of subjective gain.
evidence_quote: this WTP is about 60e/year per c.u. since this corresponds to the subjective loss below when a majority accepts the policy.
statistical_or_result_detail: Implied WTP ≈ 60 €/year per consumption unit; acceptance threshold corresponds to subjective loss tolerated.
notes: WTP is computed conditionally on believing the policy’s environmental effectiveness.

title: Yellow Vests, Pessimistic Beliefs, and Carbon Tax Aversion
authors: Thomas Douenne; Adrien Fabre
raw_determinant: pessimistic bias about net gains (overestimate losses)
definition: Respondents overestimate the carbon tax & dividend’s negative impact on their own purchasing power (pessimistic subjective net-gain bias).
mechanism: Overestimating personal costs makes the reform unattractive, shifting attitudes toward opposition to the carbon tax & dividend.
exact_tax_policy_context: Comparison of subjective vs objective household-specific net gains for the tax & dividend; bias is linked to opposition intensity.
outcome_variable: Opposition to the tax & dividend reform (disapproval of the reform).
evidence_quote: the people most opposed to the policy, and in particular those that support the Yellow Vests, are the most biased, i.e., the most inclined to overestimate their losses.
statistical_or_result_detail: Median gap objective vs subjective gains: 116€; 89% underestimate purchasing-power gains for the tax & dividend.
notes: Bias defined using household-specific objective net gains computed from official data; stronger negative bias aligns with greater opposition.

title: What explains public support for climate policies? A review of empirical and experimental studies
authors: Stefan Drews & Jeroen C.J.M. van den Bergh
geographic_scope: Mostly North America, Australia, and Europe
raw_determinant: people approved ... subsidies ... or expansion of public transportation. To a significantly lesser degree they supported two types of CO2 taxation
standardized_candidate_label: Prefer pull measures over coercive CO2 taxation
definition: CO2 taxes (push measures) are less supported when compared with non-coercive pull alternatives such as subsidies and expanded public transport.
mechanism: Coercive taxes are perceived as more costly and freedom-restricting, lowering willingness to support CO2 taxation.
exact_tax_policy_context: Swiss evidence comparing pull measures (subsidies/public transport) vs two CO2-tax types (push) (Tobler et al., 2012).
outcome_variable: Support/policy preference for CO2 taxation
evidence_quote: subsidies... public transportation... To a significantly lesser degree they supported two types of CO2 taxation
notes: Lower acceptability for tax-based push measures vs pull alternatives.

title: What explains public support for climate policies? A review of empirical and experimental studies
authors: Stefan Drews & Jeroen C.J.M. van den Bergh
geographic_scope: Mostly North America, Australia, and Europe
raw_determinant: were more likely to support a fuel tax if they believed the tax was going to reduce driving and exert a positive effect on climate change
standardized_candidate_label: Perceived fuel-tax effectiveness (reduces driving; helps climate)
definition: Fuel-tax support increases when citizens believe the tax reduces driving and improves climate outcomes.
mechanism: Perceived effectiveness/benefits make the tax more likely to achieve the intended behavioural change and climate mitigation.
exact_tax_policy_context: Norwegian sample: support for a fuel tax conditional on beliefs that it reduces driving and benefits climate (Kallbekken & Sælen, 2011).
outcome_variable: Support for fuel tax
evidence_quote: more likely to support a fuel tax if they believed... reduce driving... and exert a positive effect on climate change
page_or_section: 3.2. Perceived benefits and effectiveness of policy

title: What explains public support for climate policies? A review of empirical and experimental studies
authors: Stefan Drews & Jeroen C.J.M. van den Bergh
geographic_scope: Mostly North America, Australia, and Europe
raw_determinant: Perceived effectiveness best predicted people’s policy choices... more effective... than a carbon tax
definition: When carbon-related policies are perceived as more effective, citizens choose/support them more, including carbon taxes.
mechanism: Policy choices track perceived efficacy; carbon-tax support rises when the tax is viewed as effective versus alternatives.
exact_tax_policy_context: International survey comparing general environmental, carbon, and geoengineering policies (Bostrom et al., 2012).
outcome_variable: Policy choice/support involving carbon tax
evidence_quote: Perceived effectiveness best predicted people’s policy choices... more effective... than a carbon tax
page_or_section: 3.2. Perceived benefits and effectiveness of policy
notes: Effect is relative (effectiveness vs alternatives including carbon tax).

title: What explains public support for climate policies? A review of empirical and experimental studies
authors: Stefan Drews & Jeroen C.J.M. van den Bergh
geographic_scope: Mostly North America, Australia, and Europe
raw_determinant: overestimate the costs of taxes and underestimate the ‘hidden’ costs of alternative policies
standardized_candidate_label: Cost misperceptions about taxes reduce support
definition: Overestimating the costs of taxes (and underestimating hidden costs of alternatives) lowers support for tax-based mitigation.
mechanism: Biased beliefs inflate perceived personal burden of carbon/fuel taxes and make substitutes look cheaper.
exact_tax_policy_context: Survey evidence that respondents may misperceive tax costs versus alternatives (e.g., Jagers & Hammar, 2009).
outcome_variable: Support for tax-based climate policy
evidence_quote: people may overestimate the costs of taxes and underestimate the ‘hidden’ costs of alternative policies

title: What explains public support for climate policies? A review of empirical and experimental studies
authors: Stefan Drews & Jeroen C.J.M. van den Bergh
geographic_scope: Mostly North America, Australia, and Europe
raw_determinant: distribution of costs is progressive... richer members... pay a larger share and potential revenues are redistributed
definition: Tax support increases when burdens are progressive and revenues are redistributed to poorer or more vulnerable groups.
mechanism: Perceived fairness/inequality reduction improves legitimacy of the tax burden and acceptance of paying costs.
exact_tax_policy_context: Cross-country evidence on domestic climate policy support where progressive distribution and revenue redistribution increase acceptability (e.g., Brannlund & Persson, 2012).
outcome_variable: Support for tax-financed climate mitigation
evidence_quote: more support if the distribution of costs is progressive... richer members... pay a larger share... revenues are redistributed
notes: Presented as general domestic policy evidence; revenue redistribution is central.

title: What explains public support for climate policies? A review of empirical and experimental studies
authors: Stefan Drews & Jeroen C.J.M. van den Bergh
geographic_scope: Mostly North America, Australia, and Europe
raw_determinant: support for fairness principles ‘equality’ and ‘need’... strongly positive impact on tax support
definition: Fuel-tax support rises when citizens endorse equality (equal reductions) and need (those who need to emit reduce less) as fairness principles.
mechanism: Fairness-consistent tax rules increase moral acceptance and perceived equity of emission-reduction burdens.
exact_tax_policy_context: Swedish attitudes toward fuel taxation mapped to fairness principles (Hammar & Jagers, 2007).
outcome_variable: Fuel-tax support
evidence_quote: ‘equality’ and ‘need’... strongly positive impact on tax support

title: What explains public support for climate policies? A review of empirical and experimental studies
authors: Stefan Drews & Jeroen C.J.M. van den Bergh
geographic_scope: Mostly North America, Australia, and Europe
raw_determinant: WTP... decrease if general taxpayers are assigned great responsibility... increase if industry and energy users are targeted
standardized_candidate_label: Responsibility assignment: taxpayers vs industry/energy users
definition: Willingness to pay for carbon/fuel mitigation decreases when general taxpayers are assigned major responsibility rather than industry/energy users.
mechanism: Perceived burden-sharing shifts personal costs and fairness; public-responsibility framing reduces acceptability and WTP.
exact_tax_policy_context: WTP changes based on who is assigned responsibility (Cai, Cameron, & Gerdes, 2010).
outcome_variable: Willingness to pay for mitigation under tax-like policy framing
evidence_quote: WTP... decrease if general taxpayers are assigned great responsibility... increase if industry and energy users are targeted
notes: Direction refers to taxpayers-responsibility framing.

title: What explains public support for climate policies? A review of empirical and experimental studies
authors: Stefan Drews & Jeroen C.J.M. van den Bergh
geographic_scope: Mostly North America, Australia, and Europe
raw_determinant: acceptability of a carbon tax can vary between 68% and 48% depending on whether revenues are earmarked... or go directly to the general tax budget
standardized_candidate_label: Carbon-tax revenue earmarking vs general budget
definition: Carbon-tax acceptability is higher when revenues are earmarked for specific purposes rather than allocated to the general tax budget.
mechanism: Earmarking increases perceived transparency and benefits, improving trust and willingness to support the carbon tax.
exact_tax_policy_context: UK choice experiment: carbon-tax acceptability 68% vs 48% based on earmarking vs general budget (Bristow et al., 2010).
outcome_variable: Carbon tax acceptability
evidence_quote: acceptability of a carbon tax can vary between 68% and 48% depending on whether revenues are earmarked... or go... to the general tax budget
statistical_or_result_detail: 68% acceptability with earmarking vs 48% when revenues go to general tax budget.

title: What explains public support for climate policies? A review of empirical and experimental studies
authors: Stefan Drews & Jeroen C.J.M. van den Bergh
geographic_scope: Mostly North America, Australia, and Europe
raw_determinant: Revenue recycling... reducing VAT or income tax — increased policy support
standardized_candidate_label: Gasoline-tax revenue recycling to taxpayers (VAT/income tax reductions)
definition: Recycling gasoline-tax revenues to citizens via VAT or income-tax reductions increases support for higher gasoline taxation.
mechanism: Compensation through lower personal taxes offsets perceived gasoline-tax burdens and improves net fairness.
exact_tax_policy_context: Canada: revenue recycling back to taxpayers increases support for higher gasoline taxes (Hsu, Walters, & Purgas, 2008).
outcome_variable: Support for gasoline taxation
evidence_quote: Revenue recycling... made a marked difference: reducing VAT or income tax increased policy support

title: What explains public support for climate policies? A review of empirical and experimental studies
authors: Stefan Drews & Jeroen C.J.M. van den Bergh
geographic_scope: Mostly North America, Australia, and Europe
raw_determinant: support a rise in fossil fuel taxes if revenues are earmarked... whereas without earmarking... prefers a decrease
standardized_candidate_label: Earmarking fossil-fuel tax revenues for environmental purposes
definition: Support for fossil-fuel tax increases is higher when revenues are earmarked for environmental purposes; without earmarking many prefer tax decreases.
mechanism: Earmarking links payments to visible environmental benefits, improving legitimacy and perceived effectiveness of the tax.
exact_tax_policy_context: Norway choice experiment: support for fossil-fuel tax rises depends on environmental earmarking (Sælen & Kallbekken, 2011).
outcome_variable: Support/opposition to fossil fuel tax increases
evidence_quote: would support a rise in fossil fuel taxes if revenues are earmarked for environmental purposes... without earmarking... prefers a decrease

title: What explains public support for climate policies? A review of empirical and experimental studies
authors: Stefan Drews & Jeroen C.J.M. van den Bergh
geographic_scope: Mostly North America, Australia, and Europe
raw_determinant: policy support requires trust in government and politicians, as exemplified by the case of carbon taxation in Sweden and Norway
standardized_candidate_label: Trust in government/politicians increases carbon-tax support
definition: Higher trust in government and politicians increases public support for carbon taxation.
mechanism: Trusted governments are seen as credible implementers and fair managers of carbon-tax burdens and revenues.
exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon taxation in Sweden and Norway: trust in government/politicians linked to policy support (Hammar & Jagers, 2006; Harring & Jagers, 2013).
outcome_variable: Carbon tax policy support
evidence_quote: policy support requires trust in government and politicians... carbon taxation in Sweden and Norway

title: What explains public support for climate policies? A review of empirical and experimental studies
authors: Stefan Drews & Jeroen C.J.M. van den Bergh
geographic_scope: Mostly North America, Australia, and Europe
raw_determinant: trust in environmental scientists and environmental organizations... trust in industry negatively associated with greater policy support
standardized_candidate_label: Trust in industry reduces carbon/fuel tax support
definition: Lower trust in industry is associated with reduced policy support for climate taxation (including carbon-tax contexts).
mechanism: Distrust in industry undermines perceived legitimacy and implementation credibility, lowering support for carbon/fuel taxes.
exact_tax_policy_context: US study: trust in environmental scientists/NGOs positive; trust in industry negatively associated with greater policy support (Dietz et al., 2007).
outcome_variable: Policy support for tax-based climate mitigation
evidence_quote: trust in industry negatively associated with greater policy support
notes: Policy support is tax-related but carbon-tax instrument is not explicitly named in this sentence.

title: What explains public support for climate policies? A review of empirical and experimental studies
authors: Stefan Drews & Jeroen C.J.M. van den Bergh
geographic_scope: Mostly North America, Australia, and Europe
raw_determinant: support for a carbon tax falls when people receive information that only a minority of other citizens support the proposed tax
standardized_candidate_label: Social norms about others’ support for a carbon tax
definition: Believing that others support a carbon tax increases its acceptability; learning only minorities support it reduces carbon-tax support.
mechanism: Normative information affects perceived legitimacy and collective-action expectations, shaping willingness to support the tax.
exact_tax_policy_context: Experiment manipulating perceived majority support for a proposed carbon tax (Bolsen, Leeper, & Shapiro, 2013).
outcome_variable: Carbon tax support
evidence_quote: support for a carbon tax falls when people receive information that only a minority of other citizens support

title: What explains public support for climate policies? A review of empirical and experimental studies
authors: Stefan Drews & Jeroen C.J.M. van den Bergh
geographic_scope: Mostly North America, Australia, and Europe
raw_determinant: a majority of participants favoured a carbon tax after deliberation
definition: Public deliberation increases the likelihood that citizens favour a carbon tax.
mechanism: Deliberation provides additional reasons/arguments and can build consensus, improving acceptance of the carbon tax.
exact_tax_policy_context: Australia: study of how public deliberation affects national climate policy preferences; majority favoured a carbon tax after deliberation (Lo et al., 2013).
outcome_variable: Carbon tax favourability
evidence_quote: a majority of participants favoured a carbon tax after deliberation

title: What explains public support for climate policies? A review of empirical and experimental studies
authors: Stefan Drews & Jeroen C.J.M. van den Bergh
geographic_scope: Mostly North America, Australia, and Europe
raw_determinant: Republicans preferred to pay more... labelled as a carbon offset than... labelled as a carbon tax (no difference for Democrats)
standardized_candidate_label: Tax-label aversion: carbon tax vs carbon offset wording
definition: Wording affects WTP for the same charge: Republicans pay more when labelled ‘carbon offset’ rather than ‘carbon tax’; Democrats show no difference.
mechanism: ‘Tax’ labels trigger negative affect ('tax aversion'); political identity moderates the emotional response.
exact_tax_policy_context: US experiment: attribute framing with labels ‘carbon offset’ vs ‘carbon tax’ affects willingness to pay differently by party identification (Hardisty, Johnson, & Weber, 2009).
outcome_variable: Willingness to pay for the labelled charge
evidence_quote: Republicans... ‘carbon offset’ than... ‘carbon tax’ (no difference for Democrats)
notes: Heterogeneous by political identity (Republicans vs Democrats).

title: What explains public support for climate policies? A review of empirical and experimental studies
authors: Stefan Drews & Jeroen C.J.M. van den Bergh
geographic_scope: Mostly North America, Australia, and Europe
raw_determinant: improved public transport can reduce some public opposition to gasoline (petrol) taxes
standardized_candidate_label: Providing alternatives (public transport) reduces gasoline-tax opposition
definition: Opposition to gasoline taxes is lower when policies provide alternatives to driving, such as improved public transport.
mechanism: Alternatives reduce perceived inconvenience/behavioural costs of switching transport behaviour, increasing acceptability of gasoline taxation.
exact_tax_policy_context: Norwegian focus group result that improving public transport can reduce opposition to gasoline (petrol) taxes (Kallbekken & Aasen, 2010).
outcome_variable: Opposition to gasoline taxes
evidence_quote: improved public transport can reduce some public opposition to gasoline (petrol) taxes

title: Understanding the resistance to carbon taxes: Drivers and barriers among the general public and fuel-tax protesters
authors: Jens Ewald, Thomas Sterner, Erik Sterner
definition: Indicator for respondents drawn from the Petrol Protest 2.0 fuel-tax protest movement.
mechanism: Captures organized opposition and protest identity associated with stronger resistance to carbon taxation.
exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon tax attitudes in Sweden; comparison between the national public sample and fuel-tax protesters.
outcome_variable: Carbon tax effectiveness, policy ranking, and support for using carbon-tax revenue.
evidence_quote: being a member of the protest movement is associated with a 19% decrease
statistical_or_result_detail: -0.05*** effectiveness; -0.08*** ranking; -0.07*** collecting revenue.
notes: Sample-group indicator, not a causal treatment.

title: Understanding the resistance to carbon taxes: Drivers and barriers among the general public and fuel-tax protesters
authors: Jens Ewald, Thomas Sterner, Erik Sterner
definition: Ordinal education categories from comprehensive schooling to master’s degree.
mechanism: More education may improve understanding of carbon-pricing incentives and policy trade-offs.
exact_tax_policy_context: Swedish carbon-tax survey of public and protest samples.
outcome_variable: Carbon tax effectiveness, policy ranking, and support for using carbon-tax revenue.
evidence_quote: Support for carbon taxation is positively correlated with education.
statistical_or_result_detail: Master’s degree: +0.14*** effectiveness; +0.13*** ranking; +0.14*** collecting revenue.
notes: Higher education shows the strongest positive effects.

title: Understanding the resistance to carbon taxes: Drivers and barriers among the general public and fuel-tax protesters
authors: Jens Ewald, Thomas Sterner, Erik Sterner
definition: Disposable monthly household income categories.
mechanism: Proxy for cost exposure, but no clear association with carbon-tax attitudes in this study.
exact_tax_policy_context: Swedish carbon-tax survey.
outcome_variable: Carbon tax effectiveness, policy ranking, and support for using carbon-tax revenue.
evidence_quote: household income does not appear to matter
statistical_or_result_detail: Most income coefficients are insignificant.
notes: Null result; included because explicitly discussed.

title: Understanding the resistance to carbon taxes: Drivers and barriers among the general public and fuel-tax protesters
authors: Jens Ewald, Thomas Sterner, Erik Sterner
definition: Self-reported area of residence, contrasted with city, town, village, and countryside.
mechanism: Rural households may depend more on cars and perceive fuel/carbon taxes as less fair.
exact_tax_policy_context: Swedish carbon-tax survey.
outcome_variable: Carbon tax effectiveness, policy ranking, and support for using carbon-tax revenue.
evidence_quote: urban residence ... low levels of support
statistical_or_result_detail: Countryside vs city: -0.13*** effectiveness; -0.10*** ranking; -0.15*** collecting revenue.
notes: Rural effect is strong and robust.

title: Understanding the resistance to carbon taxes: Drivers and barriers among the general public and fuel-tax protesters
authors: Jens Ewald, Thomas Sterner, Erik Sterner
definition: Self-reported trust versus no trust in government.
mechanism: Low institutional trust reduces credibility that carbon-tax revenues will be used appropriately.
exact_tax_policy_context: Swedish carbon-tax survey.
outcome_variable: Carbon tax effectiveness, policy ranking, and support for using carbon-tax revenue.
evidence_quote: lack of trust in government ... correlate with opinions on carbon taxes
statistical_or_result_detail: No trust: -0.23*** effectiveness; -0.17*** ranking; -0.24*** collecting revenue.
notes: Largest and most consistent correlate; stronger in protesters.

title: Understanding the resistance to carbon taxes: Drivers and barriers among the general public and fuel-tax protesters
authors: Jens Ewald, Thomas Sterner, Erik Sterner
definition: Left categories on the self-placement left-right scale versus the centrist reference.
mechanism: Left-leaning respondents are more favorable to state intervention and carbon pricing.
exact_tax_policy_context: Swedish carbon-tax survey.
outcome_variable: Carbon tax effectiveness, policy ranking, and support for using carbon-tax revenue.
statistical_or_result_detail: Clearly left: +0.12*** effectiveness; +0.11*** ranking; +0.14*** collecting revenue.
notes: Left categories are more supportive than centrists.

title: Understanding the resistance to carbon taxes: Drivers and barriers among the general public and fuel-tax protesters
authors: Jens Ewald, Thomas Sterner, Erik Sterner
definition: Right categories on the self-placement left-right scale versus the centrist reference.
mechanism: Right-leaning respondents are less favorable to taxation and carbon-pricing instruments.
exact_tax_policy_context: Swedish carbon-tax survey.
outcome_variable: Carbon tax effectiveness, policy ranking, and support for using carbon-tax revenue.
statistical_or_result_detail: Clearly right: -0.08*** effectiveness; -0.07*** ranking; -0.13*** collecting revenue.
notes: Right categories are less supportive than centrists.

title: Understanding the resistance to carbon taxes: Drivers and barriers among the general public and fuel-tax protesters
authors: Jens Ewald, Thomas Sterner, Erik Sterner
definition: Belief that carbon taxation changes behavior and reduces fossil-fuel use.
mechanism: If respondents think the tax works, they are more likely to accept it and its revenue use.
exact_tax_policy_context: Swedish carbon-tax survey.
outcome_variable: Carbon tax effectiveness, policy ranking, and support for using carbon-tax revenue.
evidence_quote: those who understand the Pigouvian mechanism are the most satisfied
statistical_or_result_detail: Yes: +0.22*** effectiveness; +0.21*** ranking; +0.21*** collecting revenue.
notes: Key acceptance driver, especially among protesters.

title: Majority support for global redistributive and climate policies
authors: Adrien Fabre; Thomas Douenne; Linus Mattauch

standardized_candidate_label: partisan leaning / vote choice (Biden vs Trump)
definition: Respondent’s political orientation or partisan vote choice.
mechanism: More Democratic/left-leaning respondents are more supportive of redistributive climate taxation.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for the Global Climate Scheme (GCS): a global tax on greenhouse gases financing a global basic income.
outcome_variable: Support for the GCS
evidence_quote: The strongest correlates are political leaning.
page_or_section: Results – Stated support for the GCS; Supplementary Section F
statistical_or_result_detail: 74% support among Biden voters versus 26% among Trump voters.
notes: Descriptive voter split reported in the main text.

title: Majority support for global redistributive and climate policies
authors: Adrien Fabre; Thomas Douenne; Linus Mattauch
geographic_scope: 20-country global survey; western follow-up surveys in France, Germany, Spain, the UK and the USA
definition: Confidence in government institutions and their ability to implement policy.
mechanism: Higher institutional trust reduces concerns about implementation and enforcement.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for the Global Climate Scheme (GCS): a global tax on greenhouse gases financing a global basic income.
outcome_variable: Support for the GCS
evidence_quote: The strongest correlates are political leaning, trust in the government and perceptions that climate policies are effective at reducing emissions or in one’s self-interest.
page_or_section: Results – Stated support for the GCS; Supplementary Section F

title: Majority support for global redistributive and climate policies
authors: Adrien Fabre; Thomas Douenne; Linus Mattauch
geographic_scope: 20-country global survey; western follow-up surveys in France, Germany, Spain, the UK and the USA

mechanism: Perceiving the tax as effective increases willingness to support it.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for the Global Climate Scheme (GCS): a global tax on greenhouse gases financing a global basic income.
outcome_variable: Support for the GCS
evidence_quote: The strongest correlates are ... perceptions that climate policies are effective at reducing emissions...
page_or_section: Results – Stated support for the GCS; Supplementary Section F

title: Majority support for global redistributive and climate policies
authors: Adrien Fabre; Thomas Douenne; Linus Mattauch
geographic_scope: 20-country global survey; western follow-up surveys in France, Germany, Spain, the UK and the USA
raw_determinant: perceptions that climate policies are in one’s self-interest
definition: Belief that climate policy benefits the respondent personally.
mechanism: Perceived personal gains offset concern about tax costs.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for the Global Climate Scheme (GCS): a global tax on greenhouse gases financing a global basic income.
outcome_variable: Support for the GCS
evidence_quote: The strongest correlates are ... perceptions that climate policies are effective at reducing emissions or in one’s self-interest.
page_or_section: Results – Stated support for the GCS; Supplementary Section F

title: Majority support for global redistributive and climate policies
authors: Adrien Fabre; Thomas Douenne; Linus Mattauch
geographic_scope: 20-country global survey; western follow-up surveys in France, Germany, Spain, the UK and the USA
definition: Respondents were prompted to consider arguments for and against the GCS.
mechanism: Campaign-style exposure to counterarguments reduced stated support.
exact_tax_policy_context: US2 treatment on support for the Global Climate Scheme (GCS).
outcome_variable: Support for the GCS
evidence_quote: The support for the GCS decreased by 11 p.p. after respondents viewed a list of its pros and cons.
statistical_or_result_detail: -11 p.p. for the closed pros/cons list; -7 p.p. for the open-ended pros/cons prompt.
notes: Both closed and open-ended variants reduced support.

title: What is a fair CO2 tax increase? On fair emission reductions in the transport sector
authors: Henrik Hammar, Sverker C. Jagers
definition: Belief that people who need their cars least should reduce emissions more than others.
mechanism: Perceived need-based burden sharing can make the tax seem fairer and more acceptable.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for an increase in Sweden's CO2 tax on gasoline and diesel.
outcome_variable: Vote in favour of increased CO2 tax versus vote against.
evidence_quote: Need principle considered fair 0.409**
statistical_or_result_detail: Model 2 coefficient 0.409**; positive in full sample and frequent car users, not significant for non-frequent users.
notes: Heterogeneous by car use.

title: What is a fair CO2 tax increase? On fair emission reductions in the transport sector
authors: Henrik Hammar, Sverker C. Jagers
definition: Belief that everybody should reduce emissions by the same proportion.
mechanism: Equal burden sharing is viewed as fair and increases willingness to back the tax.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for an increase in Sweden's CO2 tax on gasoline and diesel.
outcome_variable: Vote in favour of increased CO2 tax versus vote against.
evidence_quote: Equality principle considered fair 0.615***
statistical_or_result_detail: Model 2 coefficient 0.615***; positive across all reported models.
notes: Strongest fairness predictor.

title: What is a fair CO2 tax increase? On fair emission reductions in the transport sector
authors: Henrik Hammar, Sverker C. Jagers
definition: Belief that those who pollute most should reduce emissions the most.
mechanism: Consistency with polluter-pays norms can raise tax support, especially among those less exposed to car-tax costs.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for an increase in Sweden's CO2 tax on gasoline and diesel.
outcome_variable: Vote in favour of increased CO2 tax versus vote against.
evidence_quote: Equity considered fair 0.364*
statistical_or_result_detail: Model 2 coefficient 0.364*; significant for non-frequent car users, not significant for frequent users.
notes: Heterogeneous by car use.

title: What is a fair CO2 tax increase? On fair emission reductions in the transport sector
authors: Henrik Hammar, Sverker C. Jagers
definition: High combined score across fairness principles for emission reductions.
mechanism: Stronger fairness orientation increases support for the CO2 tax increase.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for an increase in Sweden's CO2 tax on gasoline and diesel.
outcome_variable: Vote in favour of increased CO2 tax versus vote against.
evidence_quote: high ‘fairness intensity’ increases the probability of supporting a CO2 tax increase
statistical_or_result_detail: Model 4 coefficient 0.874***; middle fairness intensity not significant.
notes: Composite fairness index.

title: What is a fair CO2 tax increase? On fair emission reductions in the transport sector
authors: Henrik Hammar, Sverker C. Jagers
definition: Uses a car every day or almost every day.
mechanism: Greater exposure to fuel-tax costs and transport dependence reduce support.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for an increase in Sweden's CO2 tax on gasoline and diesel.
outcome_variable: Vote in favour of increased CO2 tax versus vote against.
evidence_quote: individuals who use a car on a daily basis are clearly less likely to support CO2 tax increases
notes: Key self-interest indicator.

title: What is a fair CO2 tax increase? On fair emission reductions in the transport sector
authors: Henrik Hammar, Sverker C. Jagers
definition: Respondent is 18 to 29 years old.
mechanism: Younger respondents are less likely to support the CO2 tax increase.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for an increase in Sweden's CO2 tax on gasoline and diesel.
outcome_variable: Vote in favour of increased CO2 tax versus vote against.
statistical_or_result_detail: Model 2 coefficient -0.559**; also negative in model 4 (-0.591**).

title: What is a fair CO2 tax increase? On fair emission reductions in the transport sector
authors: Henrik Hammar, Sverker C. Jagers
definition: Respondent is 30 to 49 years old.
mechanism: Middle-aged respondents are less likely to support the CO2 tax increase.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for an increase in Sweden's CO2 tax on gasoline and diesel.
outcome_variable: Vote in favour of increased CO2 tax versus vote against.
statistical_or_result_detail: Model 2 coefficient -0.402**; also negative in model 4 (-0.412**).

title: What is a fair CO2 tax increase? On fair emission reductions in the transport sector
authors: Henrik Hammar, Sverker C. Jagers
definition: Has at least one semester at university.
mechanism: Higher education is associated with greater support for the CO2 tax increase.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for an increase in Sweden's CO2 tax on gasoline and diesel.
outcome_variable: Vote in favour of increased CO2 tax versus vote against.
evidence_quote: Studies at university 0.485***
statistical_or_result_detail: Model 2 coefficient 0.485***; also positive in model 4 (0.457***).

title: What is a fair CO2 tax increase? On fair emission reductions in the transport sector
authors: Henrik Hammar, Sverker C. Jagers
definition: Reports sympathy with the Green Party.
mechanism: Pro-environmental political orientation strongly increases tax support.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for an increase in Sweden's CO2 tax on gasoline and diesel.
outcome_variable: Vote in favour of increased CO2 tax versus vote against.
evidence_quote: Green Party sympathiser 1.290***
statistical_or_result_detail: Model 2 coefficient 1.290***; also positive in model 4 (1.282***).

title: What is a fair CO2 tax increase? On fair emission reductions in the transport sector
authors: Henrik Hammar, Sverker C. Jagers
definition: Lives in the countryside.
mechanism: Rural residence likely reflects greater car dependence and higher perceived tax burden.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for an increase in Sweden's CO2 tax on gasoline and diesel.
outcome_variable: Vote in favour of increased CO2 tax versus vote against.
evidence_quote: Living in the countryside −0.661**
statistical_or_result_detail: Model 2 coefficient -0.661**; also negative in model 4 (-0.666**).

title: Meta-analyses of the determinants and outcomes of belief in climate change
authors: Matthew J. Hornsey, Emily A. Harris, Paul G. Bain, Kelly S. Fielding
definition: Belief that climate change is real and human-caused.
mechanism: Accepting climate change raises perceived need for mitigation, so carbon pricing seems more justified and acceptable.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for carbon tax/cap and trade.
outcome_variable: Support for carbon tax/cap and trade
evidence_quote: support for mitigation policies that place a price on carbon (carbon tax or cap and trade)
notes: Outcome pooled with cap-and-trade; no tax-only estimate.

title: Determinants of public support for transport taxes
authors: Steffen Kallbekken, Jorge H. Garcia, Kristine Korneliussen
standardized_candidate_label: Perceived fuel-tax effectiveness in improving air quality
definition: Belief that increasing fuel taxes would improve local air quality.
mechanism: Seeing the tax as effective at reducing pollution increases acceptance.

outcome_variable: Support fuel tax
evidence_quote: Effectiveness environment 0.033 [0.015]**

title: Determinants of public support for transport taxes
authors: Steffen Kallbekken, Jorge H. Garcia, Kristine Korneliussen
definition: Belief that fuel taxes hurt low-income households.
mechanism: Greater perceived regressivity lowers fairness and support.
exact_tax_policy_context: Norwegian fuel tax on gasoline and diesel; support for maintaining or increasing the existing tax (NOK 5/litre).
outcome_variable: Support fuel tax
evidence_quote: The variable distributional effect is negative and highly significant across the three models.
statistical_or_result_detail: Negative; highly significant (1%).

title: Determinants of public support for transport taxes
authors: Steffen Kallbekken, Jorge H. Garcia, Kristine Korneliussen
definition: Belief that fuel taxes hurt one's own finances.
mechanism: Expected private cost lowers support.
exact_tax_policy_context: Norwegian fuel tax on gasoline and diesel; support for maintaining or increasing the existing tax (NOK 5/litre).
outcome_variable: Support fuel tax
evidence_quote: the variable personal finance is negative and highly significant across the three instruments.
statistical_or_result_detail: Negative; highly significant (1%).

title: Determinants of public support for transport taxes
authors: Steffen Kallbekken, Jorge H. Garcia, Kristine Korneliussen
definition: Belief that good public transport alternatives are available locally.
mechanism: Better alternatives reduce car dependence and make fuel taxes less burdensome.
exact_tax_policy_context: Norwegian fuel tax on gasoline and diesel; support for maintaining or increasing the existing tax (NOK 5/litre).

evidence_quote: A one unit increase in the variable transport alternatives increases the likelihood of support of fuel taxes by 1.9% points.
statistical_or_result_detail: Fuel-tax support +1.9 percentage points; significant at 10%.

title: Determinants of public support for transport taxes
authors: Steffen Kallbekken, Jorge H. Garcia, Kristine Korneliussen
definition: Belief that authorities spend tax and fee revenues sensibly.

outcome_variable: Support fuel tax

statistical_or_result_detail: Positive; significant at 1%.

title: Determinants of public support for transport taxes
authors: Steffen Kallbekken, Jorge H. Garcia, Kristine Korneliussen
definition: General concern about harms from driving.
mechanism: Greater concern for external harms increases support.
exact_tax_policy_context: Norwegian fuel tax on gasoline and diesel; support for maintaining or increasing the existing tax (NOK 5/litre).
outcome_variable: Support fuel tax
evidence_quote: The variable concern driving is, as expected, consistently positive across the three taxes.
statistical_or_result_detail: Positive; significant for fuel tax.

title: Determinants of public support for transport taxes
authors: Steffen Kallbekken, Jorge H. Garcia, Kristine Korneliussen
definition: Indicator for female respondent.
mechanism: Women in this sample are less supportive of fuel taxes.
exact_tax_policy_context: Norwegian fuel tax on gasoline and diesel; support for maintaining or increasing the existing tax (NOK 5/litre).
outcome_variable: Support fuel tax
evidence_quote: Being female reduces the probability of acceptance of fuel taxes

title: Determinants of public support for transport taxes
authors: Steffen Kallbekken, Jorge H. Garcia, Kristine Korneliussen
definition: Indicator for having a university or college degree.
mechanism: Higher education is associated with greater support.
exact_tax_policy_context: Norwegian fuel tax on gasoline and diesel; support for maintaining or increasing the existing tax (NOK 5/litre).
outcome_variable: Support fuel tax
evidence_quote: having a university degree increases the likelihood of acceptance of these instruments

title: Climate Change Risk Perception and Policy Preferences: The Role of Affect, Imagery, and Values
authors: Anthony Leiserowitz

title: Climate Change Risk Perception and Policy Preferences: The Role of Affect, Imagery, and Values
authors: Anthony Leiserowitz

title: Climate Change Risk Perception and Policy Preferences: The Role of Affect, Imagery, and Values
authors: Anthony Leiserowitz

title: Climate Change Risk Perception and Policy Preferences: The Role of Affect, Imagery, and Values
authors: Anthony Leiserowitz

title: Climate Change Risk Perception and Policy Preferences: The Role of Affect, Imagery, and Values
authors: Anthony Leiserowitz

title: Exploring Citizen Support for Different Types of Climate Policy
authors: Ekaterina Rhodes, Jonn Axsen, Mark Jaccard

title: Exploring Citizen Support for Different Types of Climate Policy

authors: Ekaterina Rhodes, Jonn Axsen, Mark Jaccard

authors: Ekaterina Rhodes, Jonn Axsen, Mark Jaccard

title: Opinions of EU citizens about climate policy in their own words
authors: Ivan Savin; Jeroen van den Bergh
definition: Respondent is female rather than male.
mechanism: Women were less likely to mention carbon taxation as a preferred climate policy, indicating lower carbon-tax preference in the open-ended support responses.

evidence_quote: Male respondents are more likely to support ... carbon taxation.
