title: Opinions of EU citizens about climate policy in their own words
authors: Ivan Savin; Jeroen van den Bergh
definition: How informed respondents say they are about Fit for 55 climate policies.
mechanism: Higher perceived knowledge is associated with lower prevalence of mentioning carbon taxation, suggesting informed respondents were less likely to select tax-based climate policy as their preferred measure.
exact_tax_policy_context: Open-ended question on preferred climate mitigation measures; carbon taxation topic T11.
outcome_variable: Prevalence of mentioning the carbon taxation topic (T11) in open-ended responses.
evidence_quote: Perceived knowledge of climate policy ... Topic 11 Carbon taxation
statistical_or_result_detail: Coefficient for Topic 11: -0.007***

title: The public acceptance of green taxes: 2 million voters express their opinion
authors: Philippe Thalmann
definition: Affinity with left-of-center or green parties.
mechanism: Signals pro-regulation and pro-environment values; also correlates with greater turnout in the referendum.
exact_tax_policy_context: Swiss 2000 referendum on three energy taxes, especially the green tax reform.
outcome_variable: approval of the energy-tax proposals / green tax reform vote
evidence_quote: voters who expressed greater affinity with a left-of-center or ecological party voted ... 35% to 45% in favor
statistical_or_result_detail: Marginal effect on approval: +0.3501*** for green tax reform.
notes: Strongest political determinant; also raises participation.

title: The public acceptance of green taxes: 2 million voters express their opinion
authors: Philippe Thalmann
definition: Higher schooling or tertiary education.
mechanism: May improve understanding of green taxes and raise patience for long-run environmental benefits.
exact_tax_policy_context: Swiss 2000 referendum on the three energy-tax proposals.
outcome_variable: approval of the energy-tax proposals
evidence_quote: voters with a high school or university degree ... voted significantly more in favor
statistical_or_result_detail: Marginal effects: +0.2669*** to +0.2858*** for green tax reform; positive for all three proposals.
notes: Also raises turnout.

title: The public acceptance of green taxes: 2 million voters express their opinion
authors: Philippe Thalmann
definition: Voter aged 60 or older.
mechanism: Older citizens were more hostile to the proposed energy taxes, possibly reflecting stronger cost concerns or lower support for new intervention.
exact_tax_policy_context: Swiss 2000 referendum on the three energy-tax proposals.
outcome_variable: approval of the energy-tax proposals
evidence_quote: the elderly voters ... were more hostile to the proposals in proportions of 12 to 17%
statistical_or_result_detail: Negative for green tax reform and solar initiative; weaker for energy conservation package.
notes: Also somewhat higher participation.

title: The public acceptance of green taxes: 2 million voters express their opinion
authors: Philippe Thalmann
definition: Household car ownership, measured as number of cars.
mechanism: Car owners expect higher fuel-price burdens from energy taxes, especially the larger green tax reform.
exact_tax_policy_context: Swiss 2000 referendum on energy taxes on fossil fuels.
outcome_variable: approval of the energy-tax proposals
evidence_quote: voters with cars were markedly less favorable ... the proportion of approval drops by 21% per car
statistical_or_result_detail: Green tax reform marginal effect: -0.2109***; weaker or absent for the other proposals.
notes: Clear cost-exposure effect.

title: The public acceptance of green taxes: 2 million voters express their opinion
authors: Philippe Thalmann
definition: Residence in a municipality with 50,000+ inhabitants.
mechanism: Urban residents may perceive more pollution, congestion, and traffic noise, making energy taxes more attractive.
exact_tax_policy_context: Swiss 2000 referendum on energy taxes.
outcome_variable: approval of the energy-tax proposals
evidence_quote: citizens living in the largest municipalities ... were more favorable to the energy tax proposals
statistical_or_result_detail: Positive in several models, especially energy conservation package; smaller or less stable for the solar initiative.
notes: Urbanization effect.

title: The public acceptance of green taxes: 2 million voters express their opinion
authors: Philippe Thalmann
definition: French-speaking respondent or canton.
mechanism: Likely reflects regional preferences, including stronger attachment to hydropower and subsidies embedded in one proposal.
exact_tax_policy_context: Energy conservation package in the Swiss 2000 referendum.
outcome_variable: approval of the energy conservation package
evidence_quote: French-speaking voters ... were markedly more favorable to that proposal
statistical_or_result_detail: Marginal effect about +0.19 to +0.21 for the energy conservation package.
notes: Specific to the energy conservation package.

title: The public acceptance of green taxes: 2 million voters express their opinion
authors: Philippe Thalmann
raw_determinant: Priority government intervention vs. competition (1 = competition, 0 = regulation)
definition: Preference for government intervention/regulation over competition.
mechanism: Pro-regulation voters are more comfortable with state use of energy taxes to correct market prices.
exact_tax_policy_context: Swiss 2000 referendum on the three energy-tax proposals.
outcome_variable: approval of the energy-tax proposals
evidence_quote: the few voters ... with greater faith in government regulation ... were more favorable
statistical_or_result_detail: Green tax reform marginal effect for competition-coded variable: -0.2426** (i.e., pro-regulation voters more supportive).
notes: Only available in subjective model.

title: The public acceptance of green taxes: 2 million voters express their opinion
authors: Philippe Thalmann
raw_determinant: Concern with income inequality (1 = at least some, 0 = no or little concern)
definition: Expressed concern about income inequality.
mechanism: Taxs perceived as regressive or unfair become less acceptable when distributive concerns are salient.
exact_tax_policy_context: Swiss 2000 referendum on the energy-tax proposals, especially the green tax reform.
outcome_variable: approval of the energy-tax proposals
evidence_quote: Concern with income inequality lowered the likelihood of accepting the taxes by some 12% to 17%
statistical_or_result_detail: Negative and significant for green tax reform; also negative for energy conservation package and solar initiative.
notes: Distributional concern.

title: The public acceptance of green taxes: 2 million voters express their opinion
authors: Philippe Thalmann
raw_determinant: Concern with unemployment (1 = at least some, 0 = no or little concern)
definition: Expressed concern about unemployment.
mechanism: Employment-risk concerns reduce support for energy taxes viewed as potentially costly for jobs.
exact_tax_policy_context: Green tax reform with lower social security contributions in the Swiss 2000 referendum.
outcome_variable: approval of the green tax reform
evidence_quote: Concern with unemployment ... -0.6160*
statistical_or_result_detail: Negative in Table 6, but author says the effect was not convincing overall.
notes: Weak/fragile significance.

title: The public acceptance of green taxes: 2 million voters express their opinion
authors: Philippe Thalmann
raw_determinant: Higher tax rate / much higher tax rate of the green tax reform
definition: Larger tax rate and resulting price increase.
mechanism: Higher tax burdens make support harder, especially for taxpayers with greater fuel exposure.
exact_tax_policy_context: Comparison of the green tax reform with the lower-rate energy conservation package and solar initiative.
outcome_variable: approval of the energy-tax proposals
evidence_quote: The green tax reform provided for the highest tax rate by far
statistical_or_result_detail: Mainly mattered for multiple-car owners; not decisive for most voters.
notes: Policy-design comparison across proposals.

title: The public acceptance of green taxes: 2 million voters express their opinion
authors: Philippe Thalmann
raw_determinant: Mode of revenue recycling / subsidies for renewable energy and energy conservation
definition: Revenue earmarking to renewable-energy and energy-conservation subsidies rather than payroll-based refunds.
mechanism: Some voters may prefer visible subsidized environmental uses, but the paper finds no clear fairness advantage overall.
exact_tax_policy_context: Energy conservation package and solar initiative in the Swiss 2000 referendum.
outcome_variable: approval of the energy conservation package / solar initiative
evidence_quote: The energy conservation package earned the best score ... subsidies to renewable energies and energy conservation
statistical_or_result_detail: Best-performing proposal, but effect is confounded with lower tax rate and hydropower support.
notes: Design effect not isolated cleanly.

title: Overcoming public resistance to carbon taxes
authors: Stefano Carattini; Maria Carvalho; Sam Fankhauser
geographic_scope: International; evidence from Europe, North America, Australia, and Switzerland.
definition: Higher carbon or fuel tax rates, or larger personal cost burdens, reduce acceptability.
mechanism: Higher private costs make the policy feel more expensive and coercive, so voters focus on immediate losses rather than environmental gains.
exact_tax_policy_context: Narrative review of carbon, fuel, gasoline, and energy taxes in Europe and North America.
outcome_variable: public support/acceptability of carbon or fuel tax
evidence_quote: acceptability of a tax proposal decreases with the personal cost it would impose
statistical_or_result_detail: Acceptability fell almost linearly as tax rates rose.

title: Overcoming public resistance to carbon taxes
authors: Stefano Carattini; Maria Carvalho; Sam Fankhauser
geographic_scope: International; evidence from Europe, North America, Australia, and Switzerland.
definition: Perceived personal cost of the tax reduces support.
mechanism: People weigh immediate household burden more heavily than abstract environmental benefits.
exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon tax and fuel tax acceptability studies reviewed in Sections 2–3.
outcome_variable: support for carbon/fuel tax
evidence_quote: People's attitudes to carbon taxes appear to be influenced more by the direct personal cost of the measure
statistical_or_result_detail: Personal-cost sensitivity dominated environmental-objective considerations.

title: Overcoming public resistance to carbon taxes
authors: Stefano Carattini; Maria Carvalho; Sam Fankhauser
geographic_scope: International; evidence from Europe, North America, Australia, and Switzerland.
definition: Experience with a tax through trials or implementation increases acceptability.
mechanism: People update beliefs after seeing actual costs and benefits, including reduced congestion, emissions, or waste.
exact_tax_policy_context: Trial and post-implementation evidence from carbon, fuel, congestion, and waste pricing studies summarized in Section 3.2.
outcome_variable: support/acceptance of tax after trial or implementation
evidence_quote: voter aversion may abate once a policy is implemented
statistical_or_result_detail: Support rose after trial periods and after policy implementation.

title: Overcoming public resistance to carbon taxes
authors: Stefano Carattini; Maria Carvalho; Sam Fankhauser
geographic_scope: International; evidence from Europe, North America, Australia, and Switzerland.
definition: Dedicating carbon tax revenues to environmental spending increases support.
mechanism: Earmarking signals effectiveness and reduces distrust that revenues will disappear into general government budgets.
exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon tax revenue recycling and earmarking evidence in Section 4.1 and Appendix A.
outcome_variable: support/acceptability of carbon tax
evidence_quote: public acceptance for a carbon tax is higher if the use of proceeds is clearly specified
statistical_or_result_detail: Earmarking often ranked above other recycling options.

title: Overcoming public resistance to carbon taxes
authors: Stefano Carattini; Maria Carvalho; Sam Fankhauser
geographic_scope: International; evidence from Europe, North America, Australia, and Switzerland.
definition: Returning revenues evenly or targeting low-income households increases support.
mechanism: Redistribution offsets regressivity and makes the tax appear fairer and less burdensome for vulnerable households.
exact_tax_policy_context: Revenue recycling designs discussed in Section 4.2 and Table 1.
outcome_variable: support for carbon tax revenue recycling design
evidence_quote: carbon taxes with lump-sum transfers tend overall to be progressive
statistical_or_result_detail: Progressive recycling outperformed neutral or regressive designs.

title: Overcoming public resistance to carbon taxes
authors: Stefano Carattini; Maria Carvalho; Sam Fankhauser
geographic_scope: International; evidence from Europe, North America, Australia, and Switzerland.
definition: Progressive rates or threshold exemptions raise acceptability relative to flat, regressive designs.
mechanism: Visible progressivity reduces fairness concerns by shifting more burden toward higher users or higher-income households.
exact_tax_policy_context: Choice experiments on carbon and fuel tax design in Norway, Sweden, and Turkey.
outcome_variable: preference for carbon/fuel tax design
evidence_quote: respondents preferred progressive tax rates to address distributional concerns related with the tax burden on low-income households
statistical_or_result_detail: Progressive designs performed better than neutral or regressive designs.

title: Overcoming public resistance to carbon taxes
authors: Stefano Carattini; Maria Carvalho; Sam Fankhauser
geographic_scope: International; evidence from Europe, North America, Australia, and Switzerland.
definition: Using carbon tax revenue to cut other taxes is less popular than earmarking or redistribution.
mechanism: Voters often see the environmental tax and the tax cut as separate issues and distrust whether the shift will actually occur.
exact_tax_policy_context: Revenue-neutral carbon/fuel tax designs reviewed in Section 4.3 and Appendix A.
outcome_variable: support for revenue-neutral carbon/fuel tax design
evidence_quote: the least popular redistribution strategy among the public
statistical_or_result_detail: Revenue-neutral designs were generally least preferred.

title: Overcoming public resistance to carbon taxes
authors: Stefano Carattini; Maria Carvalho; Sam Fankhauser
geographic_scope: International; evidence from Europe, North America, Australia, and Switzerland.
definition: Greater trust in government or politicians raises support for carbon tax increases.
mechanism: Trust reduces suspicion that the tax is a revenue grab and increases credibility of promised recycling or environmental outcomes.
exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon-tax increase and fuel-tax reform studies in Sweden, Germany, and Denmark.
outcome_variable: support for increasing carbon/fuel tax
evidence_quote: trust in politicians is the most significant factor to support an increase in carbon tax rates
statistical_or_result_detail: Trust mattered more than self-interest in explaining resistance.

title: Overcoming public resistance to carbon taxes
authors: Stefano Carattini; Maria Carvalho; Sam Fankhauser
geographic_scope: International; evidence from Europe, North America, Australia, and Switzerland.
definition: Belief that the tax reduces emissions increases support.
mechanism: When people believe the tax changes behavior and lowers emissions, they are less likely to see it as pointless or unfair.
exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon and fuel tax studies summarized in Sections 2–5 and Appendix A.
outcome_variable: support/acceptability of carbon/fuel tax
evidence_quote: increased confidence in the effectiveness of the carbon tax to reduce emissions increases support for raising the carbon tax
statistical_or_result_detail: Perceived effectiveness was a key driver of higher support.

title: Overcoming public resistance to carbon taxes
authors: Stefano Carattini; Maria Carvalho; Sam Fankhauser
geographic_scope: International; evidence from Europe, North America, Australia, and Switzerland.
definition: Providing clear information about costs, benefits, and revenue use increases support.
mechanism: Information corrects misperceptions about effectiveness, distributional impacts, and what households receive back from the tax.
exact_tax_policy_context: Communication and information strategies for carbon-tax design and revenue recycling in Sections 5.4 and Appendix A.
outcome_variable: support/acceptability of carbon tax
evidence_quote: Providing this information reduced voters' doubt about the effectiveness of carbon taxes
statistical_or_result_detail: Information reduced demand for earmarking and raised acceptance of progressive recycling.

title: Overcoming public resistance to carbon taxes
authors: Stefano Carattini; Maria Carvalho; Sam Fankhauser
geographic_scope: International; evidence from Europe, North America, Australia, and Switzerland.
definition: Greater climate concern or environmental awareness increases support.
mechanism: People who care more about climate change are less focused on short-run price costs and more receptive to mitigation instruments.
exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon-tax and energy-tax choice experiments in Sweden, Turkey, and Switzerland.
outcome_variable: support for carbon tax / tax design
evidence_quote: Respondents with high environmental awareness were more supportive of a carbon tax
statistical_or_result_detail: Low climate concern increased sensitivity to tax rates.

title: Overcoming public resistance to carbon taxes
authors: Stefano Carattini; Maria Carvalho; Sam Fankhauser
geographic_scope: International; evidence from Europe, North America, Australia, and Switzerland.
definition: Left/green or egalitarian-communitarian worldviews increase support; hierarchical-individualistic worldviews reduce it.
mechanism: Values shape whether taxes are seen as legitimate collective action or coercive intervention, especially when redistribution is involved.
exact_tax_policy_context: Swiss energy-tax referendum and experimental evidence on worldviews summarized in Appendix A and Section 2.
outcome_variable: support for energy/carbon tax
evidence_quote: Respondents were more accepting of energy taxes if they had leftist or green affinities
statistical_or_result_detail: Hierarchical/individualistic respondents were more averse to policy intervention.
notes: Direction is mixed because different value dimensions predict opposite responses.

title: Overcoming public resistance to carbon taxes
authors: Stefano Carattini; Maria Carvalho; Sam Fankhauser
geographic_scope: International; evidence from Europe, North America, Australia, and Switzerland.
definition: Higher education is associated with greater acceptance of energy taxes.
mechanism: More educated respondents may better understand tax design, recycling, and environmental benefits.
exact_tax_policy_context: Swiss energy-tax referendum evidence summarized in Appendix A.
outcome_variable: support for energy tax
evidence_quote: Respondents were more accepting of energy taxes if they had ... higher education levels
statistical_or_result_detail: Education increased probability of a yes vote.

title: Overcoming public resistance to carbon taxes
authors: Stefano Carattini; Maria Carvalho; Sam Fankhauser
geographic_scope: International; evidence from Europe, North America, Australia, and Switzerland.
definition: Urban residents are more accepting of energy taxes than non-urban residents.
mechanism: Urban residents may face lower dependence on driving and see greater local environmental benefits or alternatives.
exact_tax_policy_context: Swiss energy-tax referendum evidence summarized in Appendix A.
outcome_variable: support for energy tax
evidence_quote: Respondents were more accepting of energy taxes if they had ... lived in cities
statistical_or_result_detail: City residents were more likely to support the tax.

title: Overcoming public resistance to carbon taxes
authors: Stefano Carattini; Maria Carvalho; Sam Fankhauser
geographic_scope: International; evidence from Europe, North America, Australia, and Switzerland.
definition: Car owners are less accepting of fuel and energy taxes.
mechanism: Vehicle owners expect greater direct fuel-cost exposure and therefore oppose fuel-related taxes more strongly.
exact_tax_policy_context: Fuel and energy tax survey and referendum evidence summarized in Appendix A.
outcome_variable: support for fuel/energy tax
evidence_quote: Respondents were more accepting of energy taxes if they had ... did not own cars
statistical_or_result_detail: Non-car owners were more supportive.

title: Overcoming public resistance to carbon taxes
authors: Stefano Carattini; Maria Carvalho; Sam Fankhauser
geographic_scope: International; evidence from Europe, North America, Australia, and Switzerland.
definition: Younger respondents are more accepting of energy taxes than older respondents.
mechanism: Younger respondents may be more favorable to policy change or more concerned about future climate impacts.
exact_tax_policy_context: Swiss energy-tax referendum evidence summarized in Appendix A.
outcome_variable: support for energy tax
evidence_quote: Respondents were more accepting of energy taxes if they had ... were younger than 60 years old
statistical_or_result_detail: Support declined with age.

title: Overcoming public resistance to carbon taxes
authors: Stefano Carattini; Maria Carvalho; Sam Fankhauser
geographic_scope: International; evidence from Europe, North America, Australia, and Switzerland.
definition: Higher-income households are more likely to support carbon taxes.
mechanism: Higher income reduces exposure to the tax burden and increases ability to absorb price changes.
exact_tax_policy_context: US national carbon-tax survey summarized in Appendix A.
outcome_variable: support for carbon tax
evidence_quote: a US$10,000 increase in a household's annual income increased the likelihood of support by 1 percentage point
statistical_or_result_detail: Income had a positive marginal effect on support.

title: Overcoming public resistance to carbon taxes
authors: Stefano Carattini; Maria Carvalho; Sam Fankhauser
geographic_scope: International; evidence from Europe, North America, Australia, and Switzerland.
definition: Fear of competitiveness losses, job losses, or factory closures reduces support.
mechanism: People worry that firms or workers exposed to fuel or carbon costs will lose jobs or relocate, hurting the wider economy.
exact_tax_policy_context: Fuel and energy tax opposition studies in Switzerland, Ireland, and Australia summarized in Sections 2–3 and Appendix A.
outcome_variable: opposition to carbon/fuel tax
evidence_quote: Loss of competitiveness and jobs and closure of factories were further concerns
statistical_or_result_detail: Competitiveness concerns helped explain rejection in several cases.

title: Understanding Public Acceptability of Climate Policies in Europe
authors: Shouyu Zhang; Susana Ferreira; Berna Karali
definition: Index of climate-change belief/concern built from skepticism items.
mechanism: Greater concern about climate change increases willingness to support mitigation taxes.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for increasing taxes on fossil fuels to reduce climate change.
outcome_variable: Carbon tax support
evidence_quote: Higher trust in the country’s political system and being more concerned about climate change are associated with stronger support for carbon taxation policy.
statistical_or_result_detail: β=0.170*** in Table 2.
notes: Also positive in ordinal probit.

title: Understanding Public Acceptability of Climate Policies in Europe
authors: Shouyu Zhang; Susana Ferreira; Berna Karali
definition: Index of trust in parliament, politicians, and political parties.
mechanism: Greater trust may reduce skepticism about government handling of tax revenues and policy implementation.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for increasing taxes on fossil fuels to reduce climate change.
outcome_variable: Carbon tax support
evidence_quote: Higher trust in the country’s political system ... [is] associated with stronger support for carbon taxation policy.
statistical_or_result_detail: β=0.212*** in Table 2.
notes: Largest individual-level predictor for carbon tax support.

title: Understanding Public Acceptability of Climate Policies in Europe
authors: Shouyu Zhang; Susana Ferreira; Berna Karali
definition: Index capturing preference for smaller income differences and fairness.
mechanism: More egalitarian values may raise support for redistributive or corrective climate taxation.
exact_tax_policy_context: Support for increasing taxes on fossil fuels to reduce climate change.
outcome_variable: Carbon tax support
